topics.title: Introduction to Data Structures and C++ Basics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Data Structures and C++ Basics

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Data Structure?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> A **Data Structure** is a specialized format for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data in memory so that it can be accessed and worked with in efficient ways. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEITT612)**, a data structure is defined as a logical/mathematical model of organizing data along with a set of operations defined upon it, encapsulating both the storage scheme and the algorithmic behavior.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

Imagine a **library**:
- If you throw 10,000 books randomly into a giant hall, finding one specific book becomes a nightmare (this is **unstructured data**).
- But if you sort them by **rack → shelf → row → author → title**, retrieval becomes instant (this is a **structured data system**).
- A **Data Structure** is precisely this "sorting/labeling rulebook" that tells the computer *where* to put data and *how* to find it later.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A data structure is not just *storage* — it is **storage + the rules/operations** (insert, delete, search, sort) that govern that storage.

---

## 1.2 Why Do We Need Data Structures?

According to the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** framework, the study of data structures is justified by three engineering pillars:

| Pillar | Real-world Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | Google Search indexing 100B+ pages in <1 sec using B-Trees and Hash Tables. |
| **Scalability** | Facebook's friend-graph requires Graph data structures to handle 2B+ nodes. |
| **Maintainability** | Clean separation of data and operations via **Abstract Data Types (ADT)**. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Whenever asked *"Why data structures?"* in Part A (3-mark) questions, always mention the three S's — **Speed, Storage, and Simplicity**.

---

## 1.3 The C++ Connection: Why C++ for Data Structures?

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically mandates **C++** as the implementation language for this course because C++ uniquely offers:

- **Classes & Objects** → directly maps to **ADTs**.
- **Templates** → enables **Generic Programming** (e.g., `Stack<int>`, `Stack<float>`).
- **Operator Overloading** → elegant syntax for complex structures.
- **Pointers & Dynamic Memory** → essential for linked structures (Linked Lists, Trees, Graphs).
- **STL (Standard Template Library)** → production-grade ready-made data structures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write C-style code in C++ (using `printf`, `malloc`). For KTU valuation, you **lose marks** for not using OOP features (`class`, `new`, `delete`, constructors).

---

## 1.4 Basic Terminology Checklist for Module 1

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Data** | Raw facts (e.g., `42`, `"Kerala"`). |
| **Information** | Processed data with meaning. |
| **Entity** | A real-world object with attributes (e.g., a *Student*). |
| **Attribute / Field** | A property of an entity (e.g., *rollNo*, *name*). |
| **Record** | A collection of related fields. |
| **File** | A collection of related records. |
| **Database** | A collection of related files. |
| **ADT** | A mathematical model with defined data + operations (independent of implementation). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Structures (The KTU Master Taxonomy)

Data structures are classified along **two parallel axes**:

### Axis 1: By Nature of Data
```
Data Structures
├── Primitive Data Structures
│   ├── Integer (int)
│   ├── Float (float, double)
│   ├── Character (char)
│   └── Boolean (bool)
└── Non-Primitive Data Structures
    ├── Linear Data Structures
    │   ├── Arrays
    │   ├── Linked Lists (Singly, Doubly, Circular)
    │   ├── Stacks (LIFO)
    │   └── Queues (FIFO, Circular, Deque, Priority)
    └── Non-Linear Data Structures
        ├── Trees (Binary, BST, AVL, B-Tree, Heap)
        ├── Graphs (Directed, Undirected, Weighted)
        └── Hash Tables
```

### Axis 2: By Memory Allocation
| Type | Allocation | Examples | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Compile-time, fixed size | Array, Stack (static) | "Fixed-size" |
| **Dynamic** | Run-time, resizable | Linked List, Tree, Dynamic Array | "Growable/Resizable" |

---

## 2.2 Abstract Data Type (ADT) — The Heart of KTU Module 1

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADT Definition (High-weightage in KTU):**
> An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a theoretical concept that defines a data type purely by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations.
>
> **Crucial Point:** An ADT does **NOT** specify *how* the data is stored in memory — only *what* operations it supports.

### The ADT Triad (used in KTU textbook problems)
An ADT has three components:
1. **Data Declaration** — the structure of data
2. **Operation Declaration** — the function prototypes
3. **Implementation** — the actual C++ code (hidden from the user)

---

## 2.3 Operations on Data Structures (The 5 Cardinal Operations)

Every data structure, linear or non-linear, supports these **5 essential operations**:

| # | Operation | Description | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Traversal** | Visit every element exactly once. | Printing all students in a class. |
| 2 | **Insertion** | Add a new element to the structure. | New registration in a queue. |
| 3 | **Deletion** | Remove an element from the structure. | Undo operation in a stack. |
| 4 | **Searching** | Locate an element by key/value. | Finding a book in a library. |
| 5 | **Sorting** | Arrange elements in a specific order. | Leaderboard ranking. |

> [!TIP]
> **Two additional operations** often tested in KTU:
> 6. **Merging** — combining two structures into one.
> 7. **Splitting** — dividing one structure into multiple.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Time Complexity (Asymptotic Notation)

The **High-Yield Complexity Cheat Sheet** for Module 1:

| Notation | Name | Meaning | Common Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| $O(1)$ | **Constant** | Time does not depend on input size $n$. | Array index access. |
| $O(\log n)$ | **Logarithmic** | Time grows slowly. | Binary Search. |
| $O(n)$ | **Linear** | Time grows proportionally to $n$. | Linear Search. |
| $O(n \log n)$ | **Log-linear** | Efficient sorting. | Merge Sort, Quick Sort (avg). |
| $O(n^2)$ | **Quadratic** | Nested loops over $n$. | Bubble Sort, Selection Sort. |
| $O(2^n)$ | **Exponential** | Recursive Fibonacci (naive). | Subset generation. |
| $O(n!)$ | **Factorial** | Permutation brute-force. | Travelling Salesman (brute). |

### Key Definitions (Asymptotic Notations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big-O (Worst Case Upper Bound):** $f(n) = O(g(n))$ iff $\exists$ constants $c > 0$ and $n_0$ such that:
> $$f(n) \le c \cdot g(n) \quad \forall n \ge n_0$$

> **Big-Omega (Best Case Lower Bound):** $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$ iff $\exists$ constants $c > 0$ and $n_0$ such that:
> $$f(n) \ge c \cdot g(n) \quad \forall n \ge n_0$$

> **Big-Theta (Tight Bound):** $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$ iff $f(n) = O(g(n))$ AND $f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-time systems (aerospace, medical devices, automotive ECUs), $O(1)$ and $O(\log n)$ operations are mandatory because worst-case latency must be bounded.

---

## 2.5 C++ Basics Refresher for DS

For the KTU OEITT612 exam, you must know these C++ constructs:

| C++ Feature | Role in Data Structures |
|---|---|
| `struct` & `class` | Defining composite types (nodes, records). |
| **Constructors / Destructors** | Memory management (`new` / `delete`). |
| **Encapsulation (`private/public`)** | ADT implementation. |
| **Templates (`template<class T>`)** | Generic data structures. |
| **Pointers (`*`, `&`, `->`)** | Linking nodes dynamically. |
| **References** | Clean function parameters. |
| **`this` pointer** | Self-reference in class methods. |
| **Inheritance / Polymorphism** | Advanced DS like virtual trees. |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU expects **C++ style memory management**: use `new` / `delete`, NOT `malloc` / `free`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Proving Big-O for Linear Search

**Problem Statement:** Determine the worst-case time complexity of Linear Search and formally prove that $T(n) = O(n)$.

### Step 1: Define the Operation Count
Linear Search examines each element sequentially. Let $T(n)$ = number of comparisons required in the **worst case** (element at last position or not present).

### Step 2: Write the Recurrence
The algorithm performs **1 comparison** to check the middle position, plus the work for the remaining $n - 1$ elements:

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & n = 1 \\
T(n-1) + 1, & n > 1
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Expand the Recurrence by Substitution

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + 1 \\
&= T(n-2) + 1 + 1 \\
&= T(n-3) + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
&= T(n-k) + k
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Base Case
Setting $n - k = 1$ (i.e., $k = n - 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + (n - 1) \\
&= 1 + n - 1 \\
&= n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Apply the Formal Big-O Definition
We must show $\exists$ constants $c = 1$ and $n_0 = 1$ such that:

$$
T(n) \le c \cdot n \quad \forall n \ge n_0
$$

Substituting $T(n) = n$:

$$
n \le 1 \cdot n \quad \text{(holds for all } n \ge 1\text{)} \;\;\blacksquare
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pattern:** Whenever you see "Prove that ... is O(...)" in a 14-mark question, always: (1) state the definition, (2) find the constant $c$, (3) find the threshold $n_0$, and (4) verify the inequality.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Linear DS

This is a **frequently asked 7-mark sub-part** in KTU Module 1:

| Feature | **Array** | **Linked List** |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Layout** | Contiguous | Non-contiguous (scattered) |
| **Size** | Fixed at compile-time | Dynamic, runtime-resizable |
| **Access** | $O(1)$ random access | $O(n)$ sequential access only |
| **Insertion at head** | $O(n)$ (shift all) | $O(1)$ (just rewire pointers) |
| **Deletion at head** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Memory Overhead** | None extra | Extra pointer per node |
| **Cache Friendliness** | Excellent (spatial locality) | Poor (random memory jumps) |
| **Use Case** | Static, frequent random access | Dynamic, frequent insertions |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Space Complexity of a Singly Linked List Node

A typical C++ node is defined as:

```cpp
class Node {
public:
    int data;      // 4 bytes (typical)
    Node* next;    // 8 bytes (on 64-bit)
};
```

The **space per node** in Big-O notation is:

$$
S_{\text{node}} = O(1) \quad \text{(constant per node)}
$$

For a list of $n$ nodes, the **total space** is:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = n \cdot S_{\text{node}} + S_{\text{head pointer}} = O(n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the array has $O(n)$ space too, but the array does NOT have the $O(n)$ pointer overhead — this is why arrays win on memory efficiency for purely static data.

---

## 3.4 ADT Specification Example: The **STACK** ADT (KTU Classic)

A stack is an ADT with LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) behavior. The formal specification:

### Data Component
A linear, ordered sequence of items: $\text{Stack} = (a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{top})$, where $top$ is the index of the topmost element.

### Operations Component
| Operation | Precondition | Postcondition | KTU Signature |
|---|---|---|---|
| `push(x)` | Stack not full | $top \leftarrow top + 1$; $S[top] \leftarrow x$ | `void push(T x)` |
| `pop()` | Stack not empty | $x \leftarrow S[top]$; $top \leftarrow top - 1$ | `T pop()` |
| `peek()` / `top()` | Stack not empty | Returns $S[top]$ without removing | `T peek()` |
| `isEmpty()` | — | Returns `true` if $top = 0$ | `bool isEmpty()` |
| `isFull()` | — | Returns `true` if $top = n$ | `bool isFull()` |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** When asked to "Define the ADT of a Stack/Queue," always include **Data + Operations** separately, and mention **Error Conditions** (`Stack Overflow`, `Stack Underflow`). Examiners allocate 2 marks for these error conditions.

---

## 3.5 Time-Space Tradeoff (The Golden Rule of DS Design)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Principle:** *You cannot simultaneously achieve minimum time and minimum space complexity — improving one often degrades the other.*
>
> **Example:** A **Hash Table** uses extra $O(n)$ space to achieve $O(1)$ average search time. Without the hash array, you'd need $O(n)$ search time.

Engineering decisions in data structure selection are based on:
- **Hardware constraints** (embedded systems → prefer low space).
- **Latency requirements** (real-time systems → prefer low time).
- **Data volume** (Big Data → prefer cache-friendly structures).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Diagram of Data Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Structures] --> B[Primitive]
    A --> C[Non-Primitive]
    B --> B1[Integer]
    B --> B2[Float]
    B --> B3[Character]
    B --> B4[Boolean]
    C --> D[Linear]
    C --> E[Non-Linear]
    D --> D1[Static Linear: Array]
    D --> D2[Dynamic Linear: Linked List]
    D --> D3[Stack LIFO]
    D --> D4[Queue FIFO]
    D --> D5[Deque Double Ended]
    E --> E1[Trees Binary BST AVL]
    E --> E2[Heap Min Max]
    E --> E3[Graphs Directed Undirected]
    E --> E4[Hash Table Chaining Probing]
    D1 -.->|Contiguous Memory| F[Memory Layout]
    D2 -.->|Scattered Memory| F
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 ADT Architecture Flow (The KTU-Exam Blueprint)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[User Programmer] -->|Uses| API[Public Interface Operations]
    API --> H{Encapsulation Boundary}
    H -->|Hidden From User| IMP[Implementation Details]
    IMP --> MEM[Memory Storage Array Pointer etc]
    API -->|Insert| OP1[push insert enqueue]
    API -->|Delete| OP2[pop delete dequeue]
    API -->|Access| OP3[peek top search]
    API -->|Utility| OP4[isEmpty isFull size]
    style U fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style API fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style IMP fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style MEM fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The user only sees the *Public Interface* (API). The implementation is hidden behind the **encapsulation boundary** — this is the **core philosophy of ADT** and a guaranteed KTU question.

## 4.3 Array vs Linked List Memory Layout (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ARR[Array: Contiguous Memory]
        A0[Base 1000] --> A1[1004 int]
        A1 --> A2[1008 int]
        A2 --> A3[1012 int]
        A3 --> A4[1016 int]
        A4 --> A5[1020 int]
    end
    subgraph LL[Linked List: Scattered Memory]
        L0[NodeA addr 2000] -->|next ptr| L1[NodeB addr 4500]
        L1 -->|next ptr| L2[NodeC addr 1100]
        L2 -->|next ptr| L3[NodeD addr 3200]
        L3 -->|next=NULL| L4[End]
    end
    style ARR fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000
    style LL fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Flow of Operations on a Generic Data Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start]) --> Init[Initialize Structure]
    Init --> Choice{User Choice}
    Choice -->|1| Op1[Insertion Operation]
    Choice -->|2| Op2[Deletion Operation]
    Choice -->|3| Op3[Searching Operation]
    Choice -->|4| Op4[Traversal Operation]
    Choice -->|5| Op5[Sorting Operation]
    Choice -->|6| Op6[Display Structure]
    Op1 --> Check1{Overflow Check}
    Check1 -->|No| I1[Insert Element]
    Check1 -->|Yes| Err1[Print Overflow Error]
    Op2 --> Check2{Underflow Check}
    Check2 -->|No| I2[Delete Element]
    Check2 -->|Yes| Err2[Print Underflow Error]
    Op3 --> I3[Return Found Position or -1]
    Op4 --> I4[Visit Each Element Once]
    Op5 --> I5[Reorder Elements by Key]
    Op6 --> I6[Print All Elements]
    I1 --> Choice
    I2 --> Choice
    I3 --> Choice
    I4 --> Choice
    I5 --> Choice
    I6 --> Choice
    Err1 --> Choice
    Err2 --> Choice
    Choice -->|0| End([End])
    style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style End fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style Choice fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style Err1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style Err2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 2-mark short answers (weightage scaled to 3 marks).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with **internal choice** (a) and (b) sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a data structure. Differentiate between primitive and non-primitive data structures with examples.*

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A data structure is a particular way of organizing and storing data in a computer so that it can be accessed and modified efficiently. It defines the logical and mathematical model of data along with the set of operations allowed on it.

**Difference Table (2 marks):**

| Basis | Primitive | Non-Primitive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Basic, built-in data types provided by the language. | Derived from primitive types. |
| **Storage** | Single value storage. | Stores multiple values (homogeneous/heterogeneous). |
| **Examples** | `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool` | Array, Linked List, Stack, Tree, Graph |
| **Complexity** | Simple, no user-defined structure. | Complex, often user-defined. |

---

### **Question 2:** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is an Abstract Data Type (ADT)? Explain with an example.*

### **Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user of the data, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does **not** specify how the data is stored in memory — it only specifies *what* operations are supported.

**Example (1 mark):** A **Stack** is an ADT that supports:
- `push(x)` — inserts element $x$ on top.
- `pop()` — removes and returns the top element.
- `peek()` — returns the top without removing.
- `isEmpty()` — checks if stack is empty.

The user can perform these operations without knowing whether the stack is implemented using an array or a linked list internally.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you are given an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**. Attempt either one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the classification of data structures in detail with a neat diagram. Distinguish between linear and non-linear data structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the ADT specification of a **Stack**. Implement a Stack ADT using a C++ class with array-based implementation. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)

The classification of data structures proceeds along two primary axes:

**1. Primitive vs Non-Primitive** (2 marks)
- **Primitive:** Basic, language-supported types (`int`, `float`, `char`, `bool`).
- **Non-Primitive:** User-defined, derived from primitives.
  - **Linear:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue.
  - **Non-Linear:** Trees, Graphs, Heaps.

**2. Linear vs Non-Linear (5 marks)** — Use the table below:

| Basis | Linear Data Structure | Non-Linear Data Structure |
|---|---|---|
| **Arrangement** | Elements arranged in a linear sequence. | Elements arranged in a hierarchical/networked manner. |
| **Levels** | Single level. | Multiple levels. |
| **Traversal** | Sequential (single pass). | Multi-level (e.g., DFS, BFS). |
| **Implementation** | Simple, often array-based. | Complex, often pointer-based. |
| **Examples** | Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue | Tree, Graph, Heap, Hash Table |
| **Time Complexity** | Mostly $O(n)$ traversal. | Often $O(\log n)$ or $O(V+E)$ traversal. |
| **Memory** | Contiguous or simple pointer chain. | Complex pointer networks. |
| **Use Case** | Simple data with a single relationship. | Real-world networks (social, road, family tree). |

#### Part (b) — Stack ADT (7 marks)

**ADT Specification (3 marks):**
```
DATA: A linear sequence S[0..n-1] of elements + integer top
OPERATIONS:
    push(x)  : Inserts x on top of stack.  [Pre: top < n-1;  Post: top ← top+1, S[top] ← x]
    pop()    : Removes and returns top.    [Pre: top ≥ 0;  Post: returns S[top], top ← top-1]
    peek()   : Returns top without removing. [Pre: top ≥ 0]
    isEmpty(): Returns true if top = -1.
    isFull() : Returns true if top = n-1.
```

**C++ Implementation (4 marks):**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Stack {
private:
    int top;
    int capacity;
    int* arr;          // dynamic array
public:
    Stack(int size) {                  // Constructor
        capacity = size;
        arr = new int[capacity];
        top = -1;
    }
    ~Stack() { delete[] arr; }         // Destructor

    void push(int x) {
        if (isFull()) {
            cout << "Stack Overflow!" << endl;
            return;
        }
        arr[++top] = x;
    }

    int pop() {
        if (isEmpty()) {
            cout << "Stack Underflow!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        return arr[top--];
    }

    int peek() {
        if (isEmpty()) {
            cout << "Stack is empty!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        return arr[top];
    }

    bool isEmpty() { return top == -1; }
    bool isFull()  { return top == capacity - 1; }
};
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the term **Algorithm**. List and explain the characteristics of a good algorithm. Discuss asymptotic notations (Big-O, Big-Ω, Big-Θ) with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Implement a C++ class for a **dynamic array** (a simple `Vector` class) supporting insertion, deletion at any position, and display operations. Analyze the time complexity of each operation. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Algorithms & Asymptotic Notations (7 marks)

**Algorithm Definition (1 mark):** A finite, well-defined sequence of unambiguous instructions for solving a class of problems or performing a computation in a bounded amount of time.

**Characteristics of a Good Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. **Input** — Zero or more well-defined inputs.
2. **Output** — At least one output with a defined relation to input.
3. **Definiteness** — Each step is unambiguous and precise.
4. **Finiteness** — Terminates after a finite number of steps.
5. **Effectiveness** — Every operation must be basic enough to be done exactly and in finite time.
6. **Efficiency** — Optimal time and space complexity.

**Asymptotic Notations (3 marks):**
- **Big-O $O(g(n))$:** Upper bound (worst case). $f(n) \le c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$. *Example:* Linear Search → $O(n)$.
- **Big-Omega $\Omega(g(n))$:** Lower bound (best case). $f(n) \ge c \cdot g(n)$ for $n \ge n_0$. *Example:* Best case of Linear Search (element at position 1) → $\Omega(1)$.
- **Big-Theta $\Theta(g(n))$:** Tight bound (average case). $f(n) = c_1 \cdot g(n)$ to $c_2 \cdot g(n)$. *Example:* Best, worst, and average of Linear Search all $\Theta(n)$ (because average $= n/2 = \Theta(n)$).

#### Part (b) — Dynamic Array Class (7 marks)

**Analysis Table (1 mark):**

| Operation | Time Complexity | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Insertion at end | $O(1)$ amortized | Direct index write. |
| Insertion at position $k$ | $O(n)$ | Shift elements right. |
| Deletion at position $k$ | $O(n)$ | Shift elements left. |
| Access by index | $O(1)$ | Direct pointer arithmetic. |
| Display all | $O(n)$ | Traverse all elements. |

**C++ Implementation (6 marks):**
```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

class Vector {
private:
    int* data;
    int size;
    int capacity;
public:
    Vector() {
        data = new int[1];
        size = 0;
        capacity = 1;
    }
    ~Vector() { delete[] data; }

    // Insert at a given position
    void insertAt(int pos, int value) {
        if (pos < 0 || pos > size) {
            cout << "Invalid position!" << endl;
            return;
        }
        if (size == capacity) {
            capacity *= 2;                      // Double capacity
            int* newData = new int[capacity];
            for (int i = 0; i < size; i++)
                newData[i] = data[i];           // Copy old elements
            delete[] data;
            data = newData;
        }
        for (int i = size; i > pos; i--)
            data[i] = data[i - 1];              // Shift right
        data[pos] = value;
        size++;
    }

    // Delete at a given position
    int deleteAt(int pos) {
        if (pos < 0 || pos >= size) {
            cout << "Invalid position!" << endl;
            return -1;
        }
        int removed = data[pos];
        for (int i = pos; i < size - 1; i++)
            data[i] = data[i + 1];              // Shift left
        size--;
        return removed;
    }

    // Display all elements
    void display() {
        if (size == 0) {
            cout << "[ ]" << endl;
            return;
        }
        cout << "[ ";
        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++)
            cout << data[i] << " ";
        cout << "]" << endl;
    }
};
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing ADT with Data Structure:** ADT = logical concept; Data Structure = physical implementation. Students mix these up and lose **2 marks** in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 2. **Forgetting error conditions:** In Stack ADT questions, you MUST mention `Stack Overflow` and `Stack Underflow`. Omission costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using `malloc`/`free` instead of `new`/`delete`:** Triggers 1-mark penalty in any C++ DS code.
> 4. **No destructor:** In classes with dynamic memory, failing to write a destructor will lose **1 mark** under "Memory Management" criteria.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in classification questions:** A neat labeled diagram of classification is worth **2 marks**. Omitting it can drop your score from full to 70%.
> 6. **Not stating base case in Big-O proofs:** Always end with $\blacksquare$ or "Q.E.D." after the inequality verification.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 1 — 5-Minute Rapid Revision:**

- [x] **Data Structure = Storage + Operations on data** (not just storage).
- [x] Two classification axes: (1) Primitive vs Non-Primitive, (2) Linear vs Non-Linear.
- [x] Primitive types: `int`, `float`, `char`, `bool`, `double`.
- [x] Linear DS: Array, Linked List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO).
- [x] Non-Linear DS: Tree, Graph, Heap, Hash Table.
- [x] **ADT = what it does, not how**; Data Structure = **how** it is implemented.
- [x] **5 Cardinal Operations:** Traversal, Insertion, Deletion, Searching, Sorting.
- [x] **Asymptotic Notations:** Big-O (upper), Big-Ω (lower), Big-Θ (tight).
- [x] **Complexity Ladder (best → worst):** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.
- [x] **C++ essentials for DS:** `class`, `new`/`delete`, templates, pointers, constructors/destructors.
- [x] **Static DS** = compile-time fixed size (Array); **Dynamic DS** = runtime resizable (Linked List).
- [x] **Time-Space Tradeoff** is the fundamental constraint in DS design.
- [x] **Golden Rule:** Array has $O(1)$ access but $O(n)$ insertion; Linked List has $O(n)$ access but $O(1)$ insertion at head.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Data Structures and C++ Basics**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 / OBE Aligned)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Data Structures: Definition, Importance, and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Data Structures: Definition, Importance, and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Data Structure** is a systematic, logical and mathematical way of organizing, storing, managing and retrieving data in computer memory such that it can be accessed and utilized **efficiently** for performing specific computational operations.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEITT612)**, a data structure is formally defined as a **named storage location that can hold a particular data type** along with the **set of operations** permissible on that data.

Mathematically, a data structure `DS` can be represented as an ordered pair:

$$
DS = (D, O)
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Finite set of data elements (homogeneous or heterogeneous)
- $O$ = Finite set of operations defined on $D$ (insert, delete, search, sort, traverse, etc.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** A data structure is a storage format that defines the way data is stored, organized, and manipulated; it also defines the relationship between data elements so that operations can be performed upon them in an efficient manner.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Wardrobe

Imagine a **cluttered almirah** where clothes, books, and shoes are dumped together randomly. To find a particular shirt, you may need to search through *all* items. Now consider a **modular wardrobe** with:
- Hanging section for shirts
- Folded shelves for trousers
- Drawers for accessories
- Shoe rack at the bottom

This is **exactly** what a data structure does — it arranges data in a *predefined logical layout* so that the computer can:
1. **Locate** a specific element quickly
2. **Insert** new data without disturbing the existing layout
3. **Delete** obsolete data efficiently
4. **Modify** values with minimal restructuring

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as choosing the wrong wardrobe design wastes your morning time, choosing the *wrong* data structure wastes the computer's **CPU cycles** and **memory**.

### 1.3 Why Data Structures Matter in Computing

| Real-World Entity | Equivalent Data Structure | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Class attendance register | **Array / List** | Sequential storage of homogeneous records |
| Family tree genealogy | **Tree** | Hierarchical parent-child relationships |
| Road map of Kerala | **Graph** | Networked nodes with weighted edges |
| Stack of plates in a canteen | **Stack (LIFO)** | Last-In-First-Out operations |
| Token system in a bank | **Queue (FIFO)** | First-In-First-Out servicing |
| Dictionary lookup | **Hash Table** | O(1) key-based retrieval |

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** *"Algorithm + Data Structure = Program"*. Niklaus Wirth's classic formulation highlights that efficient programs depend **equally** on the choice of algorithm **and** the underlying data structure.

### 1.4 Real-World Metrics Used in This Module

- **Time Complexity** — measured in Big-O notation (worst-case), denoted as $O(f(n))$
- **Space Complexity** — measured in bytes or words occupied in RAM
- **Standard Memory Units:** **1 KB = 1024 Bytes**, **1 MB = 1024 KB**, **1 GB = 1024 MB**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Data Structures (KTU Syllabus-Approved Taxonomy)

Data structures are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### A) Based on Primitive vs Non-Primitive
- **Primitive DS:** `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `pointer`, `boolean` (built into the language)
- **Non-Primitive DS:** Derived from primitive types — further divided into:
  - **Linear DS:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue
  - **Non-Linear DS:** Tree, Graph, Heap, Trie

#### B) Based on Memory Allocation
- **Static DS:** Size is **fixed at compile-time** (e.g., C++ arrays declared as `int arr[50]`)
- **Dynamic DS:** Size is **resizable at run-time** (e.g., `vector<int>`, linked lists, trees)

#### C) Based on Storage Layout
- **Contiguous DS:** Elements stored in **consecutive memory addresses** (Arrays)
- **Non-Contiguous / Linked DS:** Elements scattered, connected via **pointers/links** (Linked Lists, Trees)

### 2.2 The Six Fundamental Operations on Any Data Structure

Any data structure, regardless of type, supports a **core operational vocabulary**:

1. **Traversal** — Visit every element exactly once
2. **Search** — Locate a specific element (linear search / binary search)
3. **Insertion** — Add a new element at a given position
4. **Deletion** — Remove an element
5. **Sorting** — Arrange elements in a defined order (ascending/descending)
6. **Merging** — Combine two structures into one

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical** for Part A & Part B derivations. **Memorize the Big-O values.**

| Operation | Array | Linked List | Stack | Queue | Binary Search Tree | Hash Table |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Access by index** | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Search** | $O(n)$ linear | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Insertion** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at head | $O(1)$ top | $O(1)$ rear | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Deletion** | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ at head | $O(1)$ top | $O(1)$ front | $O(\log n)$ avg | $O(1)$ avg |
| **Space overhead** | **None** | $O(n)$ pointers | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ slots |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Asymptotic Notation Hierarchy (for comparison):**
> $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Operating Systems:** Stack → function call management; Queue → process scheduling (FCFS, Round-Robin)
- **Database Management Systems:** B-Trees / B+ Trees for indexing
- **Computer Networks:** Graphs for shortest-path routing (Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford)
- **Compiler Design:** Stacks for expression parsing, Symbol tables as Hash Tables
- **Artificial Intelligence:** Trees and Graphs for decision-making, search algorithms
- **Computer Graphics:** Quad-trees, Octrees for spatial partitioning
- **Social Networks:** Graphs (Facebook friends, LinkedIn connections)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Reasoning

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Why Data Structure Choice Affects Performance

To prove mathematically why the **choice of data structure** is not arbitrary, we compute the execution time of a search operation on two different data structures for $n = 10^6$ elements.

**Assumption:** A single CPU operation takes $\Delta t = 10^{-9}$ seconds (1 nanosecond) on a standard processor.

---

#### Step 1: Linear Search on an Unordered Array

The time complexity of linear search is given by:

$$
T_{\text{linear}}(n) = O(n)
$$

For the worst case (element at the end or not present), total operations:

$$
N_{\text{ops}} = n = 10^{6}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{\text{linear}} = n \times \Delta t = 10^{6} \times 10^{-9} = 10^{-3} \text{ seconds} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

---

#### Step 2: Binary Search on a Sorted Array

The time complexity of binary search is:

$$
T_{\text{binary}}(n) = O(\log_{2} n)
$$

For $n = 10^6$:

$$
N_{\text{ops}} = \log_{2}(10^{6}) = 6 \times \log_{2}(10) \approx 6 \times 3.3219 \approx 19.93 \approx 20 \text{ operations}
$$

Total time:

$$
T_{\text{binary}} = 20 \times 10^{-9} = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ seconds} = 20 \text{ nanoseconds}
$$

---

#### Step 3: Performance Speedup Ratio

$$
\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{linear}}}{T_{\text{binary}}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 50{,}000 \times
$$

**Conclusion:** The *same* logical task (searching) executes **50,000 times faster** simply by changing the underlying data structure and algorithm — with **zero** hardware upgrades.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is why KTU examiners emphasize data structure selection — it is the single biggest lever for software performance optimization.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Memory Footprint Comparison

Consider storing $n$ integers where each `int` occupies **4 bytes**, and a pointer occupies **8 bytes** (on a 64-bit system).

#### Static Array Memory:

$$
M_{\text{array}} = n \times \text{sizeof}(\text{int}) = 4n \text{ bytes}
$$

#### Singly Linked List Memory:

$$
M_{\text{linked}} = n \times (\text{sizeof}(\text{int}) + \text{sizeof}(\text{pointer})) = n \times (4 + 8) = 12n \text{ bytes}
$$

#### Overhead Ratio:

$$
\frac{M_{\text{linked}}}{M_{\text{array}}} = \frac{12n}{4n} = 3
$$

**Conclusion:** A linked list consumes **3x more memory** than a static array for the same number of integers due to pointer overhead — this is the classic **time-space tradeoff**.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Choose a Data Structure

| Problem Constraint | Recommended DS | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Frequent random access by index | **Array** | $O(1)$ access time |
| Frequent insertions/deletions at head | **Linked List** | $O(1)$ head operations |
| Need for LIFO (e.g., recursion, undo) | **Stack** | Native backtracking support |
| Need for FIFO (e.g., task scheduling) | **Queue** | Native ordering support |
| Hierarchical data (folders, XML) | **Tree** | Logarithmic operations |
| Networked relationships (maps, social) | **Graph** | Models arbitrary connections |
| Constant-time lookup by key | **Hash Table** | $O(1)$ average access |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of Data Structures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DATA STRUCTURES"] --> B["PRIMITIVE DS"]
    A --> C["NON-PRIMITIVE DS"]

    B --> B1["Integer"]
    B --> B2["Float"]
    B --> B3["Character"]
    B --> B4["Boolean"]
    B --> B5["Pointer"]

    C --> D["LINEAR DS"]
    C --> E["NON-LINEAR DS"]

    D --> D1["Static Linear: Array"]
    D --> D2["Dynamic Linear: Linked List"]
    D --> D3["Stack LIFO"]
    D --> D4["Queue FIFO"]

    E --> E1["Tree Hierarchical"]
    E --> E2["Graph Networked"]
    E --> E3["Heap Priority"]
    E --> E4["Trie Prefix"]

    E1 --> E1a["Binary Tree"]
    E1 --> E1b["BST"]
    E1 --> E1c["AVL Tree"]
    E1 --> E1d["B Tree"]

    E2 --> E2a["Directed Graph"]
    E2 --> E2b["Undirected Graph"]
    E2 --> E2c["Weighted Graph"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Data Structure Interaction with Algorithm

```mermaid
graph LR
    U["USER PROGRAM"] --> A["ALGORITHM LAYER"]
    A --> D["DATA STRUCTURE LAYER"]
    D --> M["PHYSICAL MEMORY RAM DISK"]

    A1["Insert Sort Search Delete"] -.controls.-> D
    D1["Array List Tree Graph"] -.manages.-> M
    M1["Bytes Blocks Pages"] -.stores.-> D

    subgraph CP1["COMPUTATIONAL PIPELINE"]
        A
        D
        M
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Time-Space Tradeoff in Data Structure Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["PROBLEM REQUIREMENT"] --> Q1["Need fast access?"]
    P --> Q2["Need fast insertion?"]
    P --> Q3{"Memory constrained?"}

    Q1 -->|Yes| R1["Use ARRAY O1 access"]
    Q1 -->|No| R2["Use LINKED LIST"]
    Q2 -->|Yes at head| R3["Use SINGLY LINKED LIST O1"]
    Q2 -->|Yes at end| R4["Use DOUBLY LINKED LIST"]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R5["Choose STATIC DS compact"]
    Q3 -->|No| R6["Choose DYNAMIC DS flexible"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Memory Layout of Static vs Dynamic Structures

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STATIC["STATIC ARRAY - CONTIGUOUS MEMORY"]
        S1["Base Address 1000: 45"] --> S2["1004: 12"] --> S3["1008: 89"] --> S4["1012: 7"] --> S5["1016: 33"]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMIC["DYNAMIC LINKED LIST - SCATTERED MEMORY"]
        L1["Node A 2050: 45 next:8500"] -.pointer.-> L2["Node B 8500: 12 next:3120"]
        L2 -.pointer.-> L3["Node C 3120: 89 next:9900"]
        L3 -.pointer.-> L4["Node D 9900: 7 next:NULL"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define a data structure. List any four operations performed on it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **data structure** is a specialized way of organizing, storing, and managing data in computer memory so that it can be accessed and manipulated efficiently. It is formally defined as a pair $(D, O)$ where $D$ is a set of data elements and $O$ is the set of permissible operations.

**Four fundamental operations:**
1. **Traversal** — Visiting each element exactly once
2. **Search** — Locating a target element
3. **Insertion** — Adding a new element
4. **Deletion** — Removing an existing element
5. **Sorting** — Rearranging in a defined order
6. **Merging** — Combining two structures

*(Any four from above — ½ mark each for operation name + 1 mark for definition = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between primitive and non-primitive data structures with examples.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive DS | Non-Primitive DS |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Basic data types built into the language | Derived from primitive types |
| **Also called** | Primary / Basic DS | User-defined DS |
| **Examples** | `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `pointer` | Array, Stack, Queue, Tree, Graph, Linked List |
| **Storage** | Fixed memory size per type | Variable, depends on design |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Can be linear or non-linear |
| **Operations** | Arithmetic, relational | Traversing, searching, sorting, inserting, deleting |

*(2 marks for table + 1 mark for examples = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1): Discuss the importance of data structures in computer science. Explain the major classifications with suitable examples.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Importance of Data Structures [7 marks]**

Data structures form the **backbone of efficient software design**. Their importance is justified along the following dimensions:

1. **Efficiency in Data Access:** A well-chosen data structure minimizes the time required to retrieve data. For example, binary search on a sorted array runs in $O(\log n)$ time, drastically faster than linear search $O(n)$.

2. **Memory Optimization:** Data structures help in utilizing memory optimally. Static structures (arrays) are memory-efficient, while dynamic structures (linked lists) offer flexibility at the cost of pointer overhead.

3. **Reusability & Modularity:** Well-implemented data structures (e.g., C++ STL containers) can be reused across multiple programs, saving development time.

4. **Algorithm Performance:** Many algorithms are designed specifically to leverage particular data structures — e.g., Dijkstra's algorithm works best with a **priority queue (min-heap)**.

5. **Problem Modeling:** Real-world problems map naturally to specific structures — family trees to *trees*, social networks to *graphs*, call stacks to *stacks*, print queues to *queues*.

6. **Scalability:** For large datasets (Big Data, ML), choosing the right data structure determines whether the system is *viable* or *impractical*.

7. **Foundation for Advanced Computing:** Compilers, OS, DBMS, AI, Networking — every advanced CS sub-domain is built upon data structure principles.

**(b) Classification of Data Structures with Examples [7 marks]**

**Classification I — Primitive vs Non-Primitive:**
- **Primitive:** `int`, `char`, `float`, `double`, `pointer`
- **Non-Primitive:** Divided into Linear and Non-Linear

**Classification II — Linear vs Non-Linear:**
- **Linear:** Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue (sequential organization)
- **Non-Linear:** Tree, Graph, Heap (hierarchical/networked organization)

**Classification III — Static vs Dynamic:**
- **Static:** Arrays (size fixed at compile time)
- **Dynamic:** Linked Lists, Trees, Graphs (size grows/shrinks at runtime)

**Classification IV — Contiguous vs Non-Contiguous:**
- **Contiguous:** Arrays — elements in consecutive memory
- **Non-Contiguous:** Linked Lists, Trees — elements connected via pointers

*(Tabulated presentation as in Section 2.1 fetches full 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2): Explain in detail the various applications of data structures in different areas of computer science. Compare linear and non-linear data structures.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]`

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Applications of Data Structures in CS [7 marks]**

| Domain | Data Structure Used | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | Stack, Queue, Linked List | Process scheduling (Round-Robin uses Queue), call stack (recursion handling), memory management (linked list of free blocks) |
| **Database Systems** | B-Tree, B+ Tree, Hash Table | Indexing for fast query retrieval, primary key lookups |
| **Compilers** | Stack, Hash Table, Syntax Tree | Expression evaluation, symbol table management, parse tree construction |
| **Computer Networks** | Graph, Trie, Priority Queue | Shortest path routing (Dijkstra's), IP lookup (Trie), packet scheduling |
| **Artificial Intelligence** | Tree, Graph, Heap | Decision trees, search trees (A*, BFS, DFS), priority-based exploration |
| **Computer Graphics** | Quad-tree, Octree, BSP Tree | 3D scene rendering, collision detection, spatial partitioning |
| **Cryptography** | Hash Table, Trees | Secure hash functions, Merkle trees in blockchain |
| **Social Networks** | Graph | Friend recommendations, shortest connection path, community detection |

**(b) Linear vs Non-Linear Data Structures [7 marks]**

| Parameter | Linear DS | Non-Linear DS |
|---|---|---|
| **Arrangement** | Sequential levels / layers | Hierarchical / networked |
| **Traversal** | Single level, one-by-one | Multi-level traversal required |
| **Examples** | Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue | Tree, Graph, Heap |
| **Implementation** | Easier, often contiguous | More complex, pointer-based |
| **Memory** | Less overhead | Higher overhead (pointers) |
| **Time Complexity** | Often $O(n)$ for search | Often $O(\log n)$ for balanced trees |
| **Application** | Simple lists, buffers, scheduling | Hierarchies, networks, AI |

*(Tabulated comparison: 5 marks. Examples & explanation: 2 marks. Total = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Vague definitions:** Writing *"Data structure is a way to store data"* costs you 0 marks. Always include the **pair $(D, O)$ formulation** or mention *operations + organization*.
> 2. **Missing examples in classifications:** Classification questions **without examples** get only 60% marks. Always pair every category with at least 2 examples.
> 3. **Ignoring Big-O:** Any discussion on *importance* MUST include at least one complexity comparison (e.g., $O(n)$ vs $O(\log n)$). Examiners explicitly look for this.
> 4. **Confusing Stack with Queue:** Stack = LIFO (Last In First Out); Queue = FIFO (First In First Out). Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **No diagrams in Part B:** For 14-mark answers, KTU expects at least **one diagram or table**. Pure prose = 1–2 mark penalty.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition:** $DS = (D, O)$ — data elements + permissible operations
- [x] **Classification axes:** Primitive/Non-Primitive, Linear/Non-Linear, Static/Dynamic, Contiguous/Non-Contiguous
- [x] **Primitive DS examples:** `int`, `float`, `char`, `double`, `pointer`
- [x] **Non-Primitive Linear DS:** Array, Linked List, Stack (LIFO), Queue (FIFO)
- [x] **Non-Primitive Non-Linear DS:** Tree, Graph, Heap, Trie
- [x] **Six core operations:** Traverse, Search, Insert, Delete, Sort, Merge
- [x] **Big-O hierarchy:** $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$
- [x] **Wirth's formula:** Algorithm + Data Structure = Program
- [x] **Key applications:** OS (Stack/Queue), DBMS (B-Tree), Compilers (Hash/Syntax Tree), Networks (Graph), AI (Search Trees)
- [x] **Time-Space tradeoff:** Arrays are time-efficient + space-efficient; Linked Lists are flexible but use 3x memory
- [x] **Why it matters:** Same task can be 50,000x faster with the right DS + algorithm combination
- [x] **Memory units:** 1 KB = 1024 Bytes, 1 MB = 1024 KB, 1 GB = 1024 MB
- [x] **Standard example for Stack:** Function call stack in recursion
- [x] **Standard example for Queue:** CPU process scheduling, printer spooler
- [x] **Standard example for Tree:** File system hierarchy, organizational charts
- [x] **Standard example for Graph:** Social networks, road maps, internet topology

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question on this topic, structure your answer as **Definition (2) + Classification Table (5) + Examples (3) + Applications (3) + Diagram (1)** — this format universally scores 12+ marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C++ Programming Basics: Pointers, References, Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** C++ Programming Basics: Pointers, References, Dynamic Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pointer?

A **pointer** is a derived data type in C++ that stores the **memory address** of another variable of a given type rather than holding a direct data value. Because every byte in RAM is numbered, a pointer "points to" the location where the actual data lives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Pointer):** *A pointer is a variable whose value is the memory address of another variable (or function) of the same base type. The general syntax is:* `type *pointer_name;` *where `type` denotes the type of data the pointer will refer to.*

**Intuitive Analogy — The House Number 🏠:**
Imagine a city where every house has a number (its **address**). The house itself stores the family (the **data**). A pointer is like a piece of paper on which you write down a house number. The paper is not the house, but you can use it to **find** the house, and even change who lives there.

```cpp
int x = 42;       // x is the house, 42 is the family living inside
int *p = &x;      // p is the paper, &x is the house number written on it
```

Here:
- `x` → the variable holding value `42`
- `&x` → the **address-of** operator (returns the house number)
- `p` → the pointer (the paper with the number)
- `*p` → the **dereference** operator (knocks on the door → fetches the value)

### 1.2 What is a Reference?

A **reference** is an **alias** (an alternative name) for an already-existing variable. Once a reference is initialized to a variable, it *becomes* that variable — you cannot make it point elsewhere, and it cannot be `null`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Reference):** *A reference is a constant pointer that is automatically dereferenced. It must be initialized at the time of declaration and cannot be reseated to refer to a different variable later.*

**Intuitive Analogy — The Nickname 🪪:**
If your friend’s name is *Rahul*, you may call him *Rah*. Both names refer to the **same person**. "Rah" is not a separate human; it's just another valid name. A C++ reference behaves identically — it is **another name for the same memory cell**.

```cpp
int x = 42;
int &ref = x;     // ref is another name for x
ref = 100;        // x is now 100
```

### 1.3 What is Dynamic Memory Allocation?

**Dynamic Memory Allocation** is the process of requesting memory from the **heap** (free store) **at runtime**, as opposed to allocating on the **stack** at compile time. In C++, this is performed using the operators `new` (allocate) and `delete` (deallocate).

> [!NOTE]
> The **heap** is a large pool of memory used for variables whose lifetime is controlled by the programmer rather than by scope rules.

**Intuitive Analogy — Renting vs Owning 🏢:**
- **Stack memory** = a hotel room booked for the duration of your stay (automatically checked out when you leave the function).
- **Heap memory** = a long-term rental apartment you lease yourself — you decide when to move in (`new`) and when to vacate (`delete`).

| Term | Keyword | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Allocate | `new` | Reserves heap memory and returns its address |
| Deallocate | `delete` | Frees the memory and returns it to the heap |
| Allocate array | `new[]` | Reserves a contiguous block of `n` elements |
| Deallocate array | `delete[]` | Frees the entire array block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight:** *“Pointers, References, Dynamic Memory Allocation (new, delete)”* is a guaranteed Module-1 topic. Mastery of pointer syntax, pointer arithmetic, the relationship between pointers and arrays, and the `new`/`delete` operators is essential for Part A and Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Pointer

A pointer variable in C++ is internally represented as an **unsigned integer** holding a memory address. Despite the address being a number, the compiler enforces **type safety**: an `int *` is treated differently from a `double *` because the pointer type tells the compiler how many bytes to read/write and how to interpret them when dereferencing.

**Size of a pointer:**
The number of bytes occupied by a pointer depends on the **system architecture**, not on the data type it points to.

$$\text{sizeof}(T*) = \begin{cases} 4 \text{ bytes} & \text{on a 32-bit system} \\ 8 \text{ bytes} & \text{on a 64-bit system} \end{cases}$$

This is because the address bus can address $2^{32}$ or $2^{64}$ distinct bytes respectively.

### 2.2 Pointer Operators — The Two Pillars

| Operator | Symbol | Name | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Address-of | `&var` | Reference operator | Returns the memory address of `var` |
| Dereference | `*ptr` | Indirection operator | Accesses the value at the address held in `ptr` |

### 2.3 Pointer Arithmetic

Pointers may be incremented, decremented, and subtracted. The compiler automatically scales the arithmetic by the size of the pointed-to type, which is the cornerstone of array traversal.

If `ptr` is of type `T *`, then:
- `ptr + n` advances the address by $n \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ bytes
- `ptr - n` retreats the address by $n \times \text{sizeof}(T)$ bytes
- `ptr2 - ptr1` returns the number of elements (not bytes) between two pointers of the same type

**The general pointer arithmetic formula:**

$$\text{addr}(ptr + n) = \text{addr}(ptr) + n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$$

### 2.4 Relationship Between Pointers and Arrays

In C++, the name of an array **decays** to a pointer to its first element in most expression contexts. Therefore:
- `arr` and `&arr[0]` are equivalent in value
- `arr[i]` is exactly `*(arr + i)`
- Pointer arithmetic is the engine that makes array indexing work

### 2.5 References — The Unbreakable Bond

A reference differs from a pointer in three critical ways:

| Property | Pointer (`T *`) | Reference (`T &`) |
|---|---|---|
| Can be `null`? | Yes (`nullptr`) | No — must refer to a valid object |
| Can be reseated? | Yes (assignable) | No — bound for life |
| Needs dereferencing? | Yes (`*ptr`) | No — used directly like the original |
| Memory overhead? | Yes — stores an address | Usually none — compiler optimizes it away |
| Syntax on use | `*ptr` to read/write | Just `ref` |

### 2.6 Dynamic Memory Operators — `new` and `delete`

The `new` operator performs **two** actions atomically:
1. Allocates memory on the heap of the requested size.
2. Invokes the constructor (for class types) and returns a typed pointer.

$$p = \texttt{new } T \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{alloc}(\text{sizeof}(T)) \;+\; \text{construct}()$$

The `delete` operator:
1. Invokes the destructor (for class types).
2. Frees the memory and returns it to the heap.

For arrays:

$$p = \texttt{new } T[n] \quad ; \quad \texttt{delete[] } p$$

> [!WARNING]
> Mixing scalar `new` with array `delete[]` (or vice versa) is **undefined behaviour** in C++ and is a frequent KTU pitfall.

### 2.7 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Pointer size | $\vert\text{sizeof}(T*)\vert = \text{architecture bits} \;/\; 8$ | Same for all types on a given system |
| Pointer advance | $\vert ptr + n \vert = \vert ptr \vert + n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$ | Scaled by element size |
| Array equivalence | $\vert arr[i] \vert \equiv \vert *(arr + i) \vert$ | Decay rule |
| Pointer difference | $\vert p2 - p1 \vert = \dfrac{\text{byte diff}}{\text{sizeof}(T)}$ | Result is in elements |
| Heap allocation | $\vert p \vert = \texttt{new } T$ | Returns `T*`; throws `std::bad_alloc` on failure |
| Array allocation | $\vert p \vert = \texttt{new } T[n]$ | Returns `T*` to first element |
| Deallocation | $\texttt{delete } p$ / $\texttt{delete[] } p$ | Must match the form of `new` |
| Null pointer | $\vert p \vert = \texttt{nullptr}$ | C++11 onwards (preferred over `NULL` or `0`) |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Linked data structures** — every node in a linked list, tree, or graph is built on the heap and connected via pointers.
- **Operating systems** — kernel memory managers use pointer arithmetic to walk page tables and slab allocators.
- **Embedded systems** — pointers map directly to memory-mapped hardware registers.
- **High-performance computing** — dynamic allocation prevents stack overflows in algorithms dealing with large matrices or graphs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example A — Pointer Declaration, Initialization, Dereferencing

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int a = 10;       // Step 1: variable a holds integer 10
    int *p = &a;      // Step 2: pointer p stores the address of a
    cout << a;        // prints 10
    cout << *p;       // prints 10 (dereferenced)
    *p = 25;          // Step 3: change value at the address p points to
    cout << a;        // prints 25 — a is also changed
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step memory trace (assume `a` is stored at address `0x7FFE10`):**

| Step | Code | Address `0x7FFE10` (a) | Pointer `p` (8 bytes) | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int a = 10;` | `10` | uninitialized | — |
| 2 | `int *p = &a;` | `10` | `0x7FFE10` | — |
| 3 | `cout << a;` | `10` | `0x7FFE10` | `10` |
| 4 | `cout << *p;` | `10` | `0x7FFE10` | `10` |
| 5 | `*p = 25;` | `25` | `0x7FFE10` | — |
| 6 | `cout << a;` | `25` | `0x7FFE10` | `25` |

**Explanation of each transition:**
- **Step 1→2:** The expression `&a` is evaluated at runtime by the compiler. It computes the machine address of `a` and stores it in the 8-byte region reserved for `p`.
- **Step 2→3:** Reading `a` directly is a memory fetch from address `0x7FFE10`.
- **Step 3→4:** `*p` is a **double indirection** — the CPU first reads the address stored in `p` (`0x7FFE10`), then performs a second fetch from that address to retrieve `10`.
- **Step 4→5:** `*p = 25` writes `25` to the address held in `p`, which *is* the address of `a`. The value of `a` is therefore modified.
- **Step 5→6:** Reading `a` now returns the updated value `25`.

### 3.2 Worked Example B — Pointer Arithmetic Derivation

Given:
```cpp
int arr[5] = {10, 20, 30, 40, 50};
int *p = arr;          // p = &arr[0]
```

Find the values of `*(p + 3)` and `(p + 4) - p`.

**Derivation of `*(p + 3)`:**

By the pointer arithmetic formula:

$$\text{addr}(p + 3) = \text{addr}(p) + 3 \cdot \text{sizeof}(\text{int})$$

Assume `arr` starts at address `1000` and `sizeof(int) = 4`:

$$\text{addr}(p + 3) = 1000 + 3 \cdot 4 = 1012$$

Reading the value at address `1012` gives the 4th element:

$$\vert *(p + 3) \vert = 40$$

**Derivation of `(p + 4) - p`:**

Two pointers to the same array are subtracted as:

$$(p + 4) - p = \dfrac{(\text{addr}(p) + 4 \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)) - \text{addr}(p)}{\text{sizeof}(T)} = \dfrac{4 \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)}{\text{sizeof}(T)} = 4$$

So the result is **`4`** (the number of elements between the two pointers, not the byte difference `16`).

### 3.3 Worked Example C — Dynamic Memory Allocation Lifecycle

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int n;
    cin >> n;

    int *arr = new int[n];          // Step 1: allocate n ints on heap
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        arr[i] = (i + 1) * 100;     // Step 2: populate
    }
    cout << arr[0] << " " << arr[n-1];

    delete[] arr;                   // Step 3: release the entire block
    arr = nullptr;                  // Step 4: avoid dangling pointer
    return 0;
}
```

**Memory lifecycle trace (for `n = 4`):**

| Phase | Stack (`n`, `arr`) | Heap Block | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| After `new int[4]` | `n=4`, `arr=0x5000` | 16 bytes reserved at `0x5000` | `arr` points to base |
| After loop | `n=4`, `arr=0x5000` | `{100,200,300,400}` | All elements set |
| After `delete[] arr` | `n=4`, `arr=0x5000` | **freed** | `arr` is now **dangling** |
| After `arr = nullptr` | `n=4`, `arr=0x0` | (still freed) | Safe — accidental deref throws on most platforms |

**Key insight:** `delete[]` deallocates the heap memory, but it does **not** clear the pointer variable. The pointer `arr` still holds the old address until you assign `nullptr`. Dereferencing it later is a **dangling pointer** error.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Stack Allocation | Heap Allocation (new) |
|---|---|---|
| Lifetime | Until end of scope | Until explicit `delete` |
| Speed | Faster (LIFO) | Slower (allocator overhead) |
| Size known at | Compile time | Runtime (dynamic) |
| Failure mode | Stack overflow | `std::bad_alloc` exception |
| Deallocation | Automatic | Manual (or via smart pointers) |
| Fragmentation | None | Possible external fragmentation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Examiner Note:** Stack memory is reclaimed even if you forget; heap memory is *leaked* if you forget `delete`. A **memory leak** occurs when allocated heap memory becomes unreachable without being freed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Program Memory Layout] --> B[Stack Segment]
    A --> C[Heap Segment]
    A --> D[Data Segment]
    A --> E[Code Segment]
    B --> B1[Local variables]
    B --> B2[Function call frames]
    B --> B3[Return addresses]
    C --> C1[Dynamic objects via new]
    C --> C2[Linked list nodes]
    C --> C3[Trees and graphs]
    D --> D1[Global variables]
    D --> D2[Static variables]
    E --> E1[Compiled machine instructions]
    C1 -. freed by delete .-> F[Free List of Heap]
    C2 -. freed by delete .-> F
    C3 -. freed by delete .-> F
```

### 4.2 Pointer vs Reference — Visual Mental Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Memory["Memory Cell at 0x1000"]
        V1["Value = 42"]
    end
    A["int x = 42"] --> Memory
    P["int* p = &x"] -- "stores address" --> Memory
    R["int& r = x"] -- "is alias of" --> Memory
    P2["*p = 100"] -- "writes through pointer" --> Memory
    R2["r = 100"] -- "writes through alias" --> Memory
    Memory -. "read" .-> P3["cout less than less than *p"]
    Memory -. "read" .-> R3["cout less than less than r"]
```

### 4.3 Dynamic Memory Allocation Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Requested: new T or new T[n]
    Requested --> Allocated: Memory reserved, pointer returned
    Allocated --> InUse: Program accesses data
    InUse --> InUse: Read and write operations
    InUse --> Freed: delete or delete[]
    Freed --> [*]: Memory returned to heap
    Allocated --> Leak: Lost reference without delete
    Leak --> [*]: Memory permanently unavailable
    InUse --> Dangling: delete without nullptr assignment
    Dangling --> [*]: Pointer still holds old address
```

### 4.4 Pointer-to-Array Decay

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["int arr[5] = {10,20,30,40,50}"] --> B["arr decays to int* in expressions"]
    B --> C1["arr equivalent to &arr less than 0 greater than"]
    B --> C2["arr less than i greater than equivalent to *(arr + i)"]
    B --> C3["arr + 1 advances by sizeof int bytes"]
    C1 --> D["Points to first element"]
    C2 --> E["Used for index based access"]
    C3 --> F["Used for pointer traversal"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between a pointer and a reference in C++. When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pointer | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `int *p;` | `int &r = x;` |
| Can be null? | Yes (`nullptr`) | No |
| Can be reseated? | Yes | No |
| Dereferencing | Required (`*p`) | Not required |
| Memory | Holds an address | Alias of existing variable |

**Preferred usage:**
- Use **references** for function parameters (especially `const &`) and return types to avoid copying.
- Use **pointers** when you need to reassign, work with `nullptr`, perform pointer arithmetic, or manage dynamic memory.

(Marking scheme: 2 marks for the table, 1 mark for usage preference.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is dynamic memory allocation? Differentiate between `malloc()` and `new` in C++.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Dynamic memory allocation** is the mechanism of reserving memory at runtime from the heap, allowing programs to handle data whose size is not known at compile time.

| Feature | `malloc()` (C-style) | `new` (C++ operator) |
|---|---|---|
| Header needed | `<cstdlib>` | None (built-in) |
| Returns | `void*` (needs cast) | Typed pointer `T*` |
| Calls constructor? | No | Yes |
| Failure mode | Returns `NULL` | Throws `std::bad_alloc` |
| Freeing | `free(ptr)` | `delete ptr` |
| Overridable? | No | Yes (`operator new`) |

**Key advantage of `new`:** Type safety + automatic constructor invocation, making it the idiomatic C++ choice.

(Marking scheme: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for comparison table.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *“In each module, Part B will contain choice — the student shall answer either Q(a) OR Q(b) of the same module.”* Hence we provide two alternative 14-mark questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the concepts of pointers and references in C++ with suitable code examples. Discuss pointer arithmetic with a worked example involving an integer array of size 5.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Write a C++ program that dynamically allocates a 2D matrix of size `m × n` entered by the user, populates it with the product of indices, displays the matrix, and properly deallocates the memory. Show the output for `m = 3, n = 4`.*

**Model Solution to (a):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    // POINTER demonstration
    int x = 25;
    int *p = &x;
    cout << "Value of x   = " << x  << endl;
    cout << "Address of x = " << &x << endl;
    cout << "Value via p  = " << *p << endl;
    *p = 50;
    cout << "After *p=50, x = " << x << endl;

    // REFERENCE demonstration
    int y = 100;
    int &ref = y;
    cout << "y via ref     = " << ref << endl;
    ref = 200;
    cout << "After ref=200, y = " << y << endl;

    // POINTER ARITHMETIC
    int arr[5] = {11, 22, 33, 44, 55};
    int *q = arr;            // points to arr[0]
    cout << "*(q+2) = " << *(q + 2) << endl;  // 33
    cout << "*(q+4) = " << *(q + 4) << endl;  // 55
    cout << "Elements between (q+4) and q = "
         << (q + 4) - q << endl;             // 4

    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Value of x   = 25
Address of x = 0x7ffe14
Value via p  = 25
After *p=50, x = 50
y via ref     = 100
After ref=200, y = 200
*(q+2) = 33
*(q+4) = 55
Elements between (q+4) and q = 4
```

**Pointer Arithmetic Explanation (board-style):**
When `q = arr` and `arr` is at address `1000` with `sizeof(int) = 4`:
- `q + 2` → address `1000 + 2·4 = 1008` → holds `arr[2] = 33`.
- `q + 4` → address `1000 + 4·4 = 1016` → holds `arr[4] = 55`.
- Pointer subtraction yields element count, not byte count: `(q+4) - q = 4`.

(Marking allocation: 2 marks for pointer demo, 2 marks for reference demo, 3 marks for pointer arithmetic with explanation.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    int m, n;
    cout << "Enter m and n: ";
    cin >> m >> n;

    // Allocate array of m row-pointers
    int **mat = new int*[m];
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        mat[i] = new int[n];        // allocate each row
    }

    // Populate with product of indices
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            mat[i][j] = i * j;
        }
    }

    // Display
    cout << "\nMatrix:\n";
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
            cout << mat[i][j] << "\t";
        }
        cout << endl;
    }

    // Deallocate (reverse order!)
    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
        delete[] mat[i];
    }
    delete[] mat;
    mat = nullptr;

    return 0;
}
```

**Output for `m = 3, n = 4`:**
```
Matrix:
0       0       0       0
0       1       2       3
0       2       4       6
```

**Explanation of deallocation order:**
Each row was allocated individually, so each row must be `delete[]`d individually. The outer array of pointers is deallocated last. This **reverse-order** cleanup is critical to avoid memory leaks.

(Marking allocation: 2 marks for correct allocation, 2 marks for population + display, 2 marks for proper deallocation, 1 mark for output.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Discuss the various pointer-related pitfalls in C++ — dangling pointers, wild pointers, and memory leaks. Provide illustrative code snippets and explain how to avoid them.*

**(b) [7 marks]** *Write a C++ program that accepts `n` integers from the user, stores them in a dynamically allocated array, sorts the array in ascending order using pointer-based access (i.e., use `*` and pointer arithmetic instead of `arr[i]`), and finally releases the memory.*

**Model Solution to (a):**

**1. Dangling Pointer** — a pointer that still holds the address of memory that has already been freed.

```cpp
int *p = new int(10);
delete p;        // memory freed
cout << *p;      // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOUR — dangling access
// Fix: p = nullptr; immediately after delete.
```

**2. Wild Pointer** — a pointer that has been declared but never initialized to a known address.

```cpp
int *p;          // garbage address
*p = 5;          // CRASH — writing to arbitrary memory
// Fix: always initialize pointers: int *p = nullptr;
```

**3. Memory Leak** — heap memory is allocated but the pointer to it is lost without freeing.

```cpp
void foo() {
    int *p = new int[100];
    return;       // p goes out of scope — array NEVER freed
}
// Fix: delete[] p; before return, or use smart pointers (unique_ptr).
```

**4. Double Deletion**

```cpp
delete p;
delete p;        // UNDEFINED BEHAVIOUR
// Fix: p = nullptr; after delete, or check if(p) before delete.
```

**Defensive Coding Rules (board exam favourite):**
- Initialize every pointer at declaration: `int *p = nullptr;`
- Set pointers to `nullptr` immediately after `delete`.
- Match every `new` with a `delete` and every `new[]` with a `delete[]`.
- Prefer **smart pointers** (`std::unique_ptr`, `std::shared_ptr`) in modern C++.

(Marking allocation: 2 marks per pitfall with code, 1 mark for prevention strategies.)

**Model Solution to (b):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;

int sortAscending(int *begin, int *end) {
    int *i, *j;
    for (i = begin; i < end; i++) {
        for (j = i + 1; j < end; j++) {
            if (*i > *j) {
                int temp = *i;
                *i = *j;
                *j = temp;
            }
        }
    }
}

int main() {
    int n;
    cout << "Enter n: ";
    cin >> n;

    int *arr = new int[n];
    cout << "Enter " << n << " integers: ";
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cin >> *(arr + i);            // pointer-based input
    }

    sortAscending(arr, arr + n);

    cout << "Sorted array: ";
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        cout << *(arr + i) << " ";    // pointer-based output
    }
    cout << endl;

    delete[] arr;
    arr = nullptr;
    return 0;
}
```

**Sample Run:**
```
Enter n: 5
Enter 5 integers: 34 12 5 67 1
Sorted array: 1 5 12 34 67
```

**Pointer-Arithmetic Justification:**
`arr + n` is the address one-past-the-end, so passing `arr` and `arr + n` defines a half-open range `[begin, end)` — a standard C++ idiom. The inner loop `j = i + 1` uses pointer stepping, satisfying the constraint of avoiding `arr[i]` notation.

(Marking allocation: 2 marks for dynamic allocation + input, 3 marks for sort using pointer arithmetic, 1 mark for output, 1 mark for cleanup.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `delete[]` for arrays.** Using `delete p` instead of `delete[] p` for `new T[n]` → 2-mark penalty, often flagged as "undefined behaviour".
> 2. **Confusing `*p` with `&p`.** Writing `&p` when they meant to dereference is a classic conceptual error.
> 3. **Mixing `new`/`malloc`.** If you allocate with `new`, free with `delete`; if with `malloc`, free with `free`. Never mix.
> 4. **Pointer subtraction misunderstanding.** Answering in bytes instead of element count for `(p2 - p1)`.
> 5. **Omitting `nullptr` after `delete`.** Examiners reward this defensive practice with bonus marks.
> 6. **Forgetting reverse-order cleanup in 2D arrays.** Deallocating the row-pointers array before the individual rows causes leaks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- A **pointer** stores an address; a **reference** is an alias of an existing variable.
- `&` is the *address-of* operator; `*` is the *dereference/indirection* operator.
- `sizeof(T*)` is **system-dependent** (`4` on 32-bit, `8` on 64-bit), **not type-dependent**.
- **Pointer arithmetic is scaled by `sizeof(T)`**: `ptr + n` advances by $n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$ bytes.
- **Pointer subtraction yields element count**, not byte difference.
- Arrays **decay to pointers** in expressions: `arr[i] ≡ *(arr + i)`.
- References **cannot be null**, **cannot be reseated**, and require **no dereferencing syntax**.
- `new` returns a typed pointer and **calls the constructor**; `malloc` does neither.
- Pair every `new` with `delete` and every `new[]` with `delete[]` — never mix.
- After `delete`, immediately assign `nullptr` to prevent **dangling pointer** bugs.
- **Stack memory** is auto-managed; **heap memory** is the programmer's responsibility.
- For 2D dynamic arrays: allocate the array of row-pointers first, allocate each row second, then **deallocate in reverse order** (rows first, pointer array last).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Abstract Data Types (ADTs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++ > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Abstract Data Types (ADTs) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Abstract Data Type (ADT)** is a theoretical computational model in which a data type is defined by its **behavior (semantics)** from the point of view of a *user*, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations. An ADT does not specify *how* the data is stored in memory or *how* the operations are implemented — it only specifies *what* operations exist and *what* they do. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > "An Abstract Data Type is a mathematical model for data types where a data type is defined by its behavior (semantics) from the point of view of a user of the data, specifically in terms of possible values, possible operations on data of this type, and the behavior of these operations." > — *Standard KTU 2024 Scheme reference for ADT.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Car Dashboard (Speedometer):** > Think of a **car's speedometer**. As a driver (the *user*), you only care about *what* you can do with it: > - You can **read the current speed**. > - You can **reset the trip meter**. > - You can **switch units** (km/h to mph). > > You **do not** need to know *how* the speedometer is built, what gears are inside, or how the needle is calibrated. The speedometer presents a clean **abstract interface** hiding all internal complexity. > > This is exactly what an **ADT** does for data: it tells the programmer *what operations are available* but hides *how they are implemented* in memory. ### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Data Type** | A classification identifying one of various types of data (int, float, char) that determines possible values and operations. | | **Abstraction** | The process of hiding background details and emphasizing essential features. | | **Encapsulation** | Bundling data and the methods that operate on that data within a single unit (e.g., a class). | | **Interface** | The set of operations an ADT exposes to the outside world. | | **Implementation** | The internal mechanism (arrays, linked lists, etc.) used to realize the ADT's promises. | | **Specification** | A formal description of the ADT's data and operations. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Examiners repeatedly test the **distinction between ADT and Data Structure**. An ADT is a *logical/ mathematical* description; a Data Structure is a *concrete implementation* in memory. --- ### 1.4 The Three Pillars of an ADT Every ADT, as per KTU syllabus, is defined by **three components**: 1. **Data (Domain):** A set of possible values the type can hold. *Example:* For a `Stack` ADT, the domain is "any sequence of elements of some base type T." 2. **Operations (Functions):** A set of procedures that act on the data. *Example:* `push()`, `pop()`, `top()`, `isEmpty()`. 3. **Semantics (Axioms/Pre-Post Conditions):** Rules that govern how operations behave. *Example:* "A `pop()` on an empty stack is undefined" or "After `push(x)`, the `top()` returns `x`." ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of an ADT — Step-by-Step Logic An ADT is constructed layer by layer. Understanding these layers is essential for KTU's 14-mark questions. - **Step 1 — Identify the Data Domain:** Decide *what kind of values* the type will represent.
