<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (2 to attempt, each ~3 marks).
> - **Part B:** 14-mark long-answer with **internal choice** (answer either Option A or Option B; each has two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between an Array and a Linked List. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Array | Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Memory Allocation | Contiguous (static) | Non-contiguous (dynamic) |
| Size | Fixed at compile-time | Grows/shrinks at runtime |
| Access | Random access, $O(1)$ | Sequential access, $O(n)$ |
| Memory Waste | Possible (unused slots) | None (allocated as needed) |
| Insertion/Deletion | Costly — $O(n)$ (shifting) | Efficient at head — $O(1)$ |

*(Any three valid points = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a Circular Linked List? How is it different from a Singly Linked List?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a variation of a linked list in which the **last node's pointer does not point to `NULL`**; instead, it points **back to the head node**, forming a closed loop or circle.

**Key Differences from SLL:**

1. **End Marker:** SLL ends with `NULL`; CLL has **no `NULL`** inside the cycle.
2. **Traversal Termination:** SLL stops at `NULL`; CLL uses a **`do-while` loop** that terminates when we return to the `head`.
3. **Use Case:** SLL suits linear sequences; CLL suits cyclic applications like **round-robin scheduling** and **circular buffers**.
4. **Risk:** CLL must be carefully coded to avoid **infinite loops**.

*(Full answer with definition + 3 differences = 3 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> For linked list algorithms, students often forget to:
> - **Update the head pointer** when inserting/deleting at the head.
> - **Handle the empty list case** (`head == NULL`) separately.
> - **Set `newNode->next = NULL`** for SLL tail insertions.
> - **Use `do-while` for CLL traversal** (regular `while` causes infinite loops).
> - **Update BOTH `prev` and `next`** in DLL operations.
> Each missing case = **1–2 marks deducted**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the structure of a **Singly Linked List** node. Write the algorithm to **insert a node at the end** of a Singly Linked List with necessary diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the algorithm to **delete a node from the beginning** of a Singly Linked List. Explain with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Node Structure (2 marks):**

```cpp
struct Node {
    int data;        // Data field
    Node* next;      // Pointer to next node
};
```

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
HEAD --> [10|next] --> [20|next] --> [30|NULL]
```

**Insertion-at-End Algorithm (3 marks):**

| Step | Pseudocode |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `newNode = new Node()` |
| 2 | `newNode->data = val` |
| 3 | `newNode->next = NULL` |
| 4 | `IF head == NULL THEN head = newNode; RETURN` |
| 5 | `temp = head` |
| 6 | `WHILE temp->next != NULL: temp = temp->next` |
| 7 | `temp->next = newNode` |

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Concept (2 marks):** Deletion at the beginning is the **simplest deletion** operation in an SLL since no traversal is required — just rewire the head pointer.

**Algorithm (3 marks):**

| Step | Pseudocode |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `IF head == NULL THEN print "List Empty"; RETURN` |
| 2 | `temp = head` |
| 3 | `head = head->next` |
| 4 | `delete temp` |
| 5 | `RETURN head` |

**Example Trace (2 marks):**

Before: `HEAD → [10|*] → [20|*] → [30|NULL]`
After deleting from beginning: `HEAD → [20|*] → [30|NULL]`
The deleted node's memory is freed using `delete`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ — constant time.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** What is a **Doubly Linked List**? Compare it with a **Singly Linked List**. Write the algorithm to **insert a node at any given position** in a Doubly Linked List. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Circular Singly Linked List** with a neat diagram. Write an algorithm to **insert a node at the end** of a Circular Singly Linked List. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **Doubly Linked List (DLL)** is a linked list in which each node contains **three fields**: a data field, a pointer to the next node (`next`), and a pointer to the previous node (`prev`). This allows traversal in **both directions**.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | SLL | DLL |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointers per node | 1 | 2 |
| Traversal | Forward only | Both directions |
| Memory | Less | More |
| Deletion given node pointer | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |

**Node Structure (1 mark):**

```cpp
struct DNode {
    int data;
    DNode* next;
    DNode* prev;
};
```

**Insertion at Position `pos` Algorithm (3 marks):**

| Step | Pseudocode |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `newNode = new DNode(); newNode->data = val` |
| 2 | `IF pos == 1` (insert at head): wire `newNode->next = head; head->prev = newNode; head = newNode; RETURN` |
| 3 | `temp = head; count = 1` |
| 4 | `WHILE temp != NULL AND count < pos-1: temp = temp->next; count++` |
| 5 | `IF temp == NULL: print "Invalid position"; RETURN` |
| 6 | `newNode->next = temp->next` |
| 7 | `newNode->prev = temp` |
| 8 | `IF temp->next != NULL: temp->next->prev = newNode` |
| 9 | `temp->next = newNode` |

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ for traversal to position, but the **re-wiring is $O(1)$**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition + Diagram (3 marks):**

A **Circular Singly Linked List** is a singly linked list in which the **last node's `next` pointer points back to the head**, forming a circular chain.

```
HEAD --> [10|*] --> [20|*] --> [30|*] --+
          ^                              |
          |______________________________|
```

**Algorithm — Insert at End of Circular SLL (4 marks):**

| Step | Pseudocode |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `newNode = new Node(); newNode->data = val` |
| 2 | `IF head == NULL: head = newNode; newNode->next = head; RETURN` |
| 3 | `temp = head` |
| 4 | `WHILE temp->next != head: temp = temp->next` |
| 5 | `temp->next = newNode` |
| 6 | `newNode->next = head` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Difference from SLL:** Step 4 uses `temp->next != head` (not `!= NULL`). Step 6 sets `newNode->next = head` (not `= NULL`). Forgetting these is the most common error in CLL algorithms.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2 — Circular Linked List Specific:**
> Students frequently:
> - Use `temp->next != NULL` instead of `temp->next != head` → causes **infinite loop**.
> - Forget to make the new node point to `head` (it will point to `NULL`, breaking the cycle).
> - Forget to set `newNode->next = head` in the **single-node** case explicitly.
> Each of these can cost **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark question.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the exam hall? Read this section.**

### ✅ Singly Linked List (SLL)
- Each node: **1 data + 1 `next` pointer**.
- Traversal: **forward only** (head → tail).
- End marker: **`NULL`**.
- Insertion at head: **$O(1)$**.
- Insertion at tail: **$O(n)$** (must traverse).
- Use when: simple linear storage, memory is tight.

### ✅ Doubly Linked List (DLL)
- Each node: **1 data + `next` + `prev`**.
- Traversal: **bidirectional** (can go backward).
- Deletion given direct node pointer: **$O(1)$** (killer feature).
- Memory overhead: **higher** (~extra pointer per node).
- Use when: backward navigation is needed (browser history, LRU cache, undo/redo).

### ✅ Circular Linked List (CLL)
- Last node's pointer → **back to head** (no `NULL` in the cycle).
- Traversal: use **`do-while`** loop stopping at `head`.
- Sub-types: **Circular SLL** and **Circular DLL**.
- Use when: cyclic processes (round-robin scheduling, circular buffers, multiplayer turns).

### ✅ Universal Concepts to Remember
- **Memory:** Linked lists use **dynamic, non-contiguous** memory (heap).
- **Head pointer:** Must be **maintained and updated** at all times.
- **Empty list:** `head == NULL`.
- **No random access:** Access is **always sequential**, $O(n)$ worst case.
- **Dynamic size:** No pre-allocation; grow/shrink as needed.
- **`new`/`delete`:** Always pair them to avoid memory leaks (write a destructor!).

### ✅ Common KTU Question Patterns
1. *"Write an algorithm to insert a node at position k."* → Must handle `k=1` (head), `k=n+1` (tail), and `k > n+1` (invalid).
2. *"Compare SLL, DLL, and CLL."* → Use the comparison table from Section 2.4.
3. *"Explain the advantages and disadvantages of linked lists over arrays."* → Dynamic size, efficient insertion vs. no random access, extra memory.
4. *"Write a function to reverse a linked list."* → Three-pointer technique (`prev`, `curr`, `next`).
5. *"Detect a loop in a linked list."* → **Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm** (Tortoise and Hare).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:**
> *"Always draw the diagram first. Then trace through your algorithm on the diagram. If the pointer rewiring looks wrong in the picture, it IS wrong in the code."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stacks: Implementation Using Arrays and Linked Lists, Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++ > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Stacks: Implementation Using Arrays and Linked Lists, Applications # MODULE 2 — STACKS: COMPREHENSIVE STUDY NOTES ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **STACK — Formal Definition** > A **Stack** is a linear, ordered data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** discipline, where insertions and deletions are permitted at only one end, called the **TOP** of the stack. The two fundamental mutator operations are **PUSH** (insertion) and **POP** (deletion). A non-mutator **PEEK** (or TOP) operation is also defined to inspect the element at the top without removing it. **KTU-Specified Stack Primitives (ADT Contract):** | Operation | Description | Pre-condition | Post-condition | |-----------|-------------|---------------|----------------| | `push(x)` | Inserts element `x` at the top | Stack must not be full | `top` increments by 1 | | `pop()` | Removes and returns top element | Stack must not be empty | `top` decrements by 1 | | `peek()` | Returns top element without removing | Stack must not be empty | No state change | | `isEmpty()` | Tests whether stack is empty | None | Returns `bool` | | `isFull()` | Tests whether stack is full (array only) | None | Returns `bool` | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pile of Plates" Intuition Imagine a stack of dinner plates in a cafeteria: - You can only **add** (push) a plate to the **top** of the pile. - You can only **remove** (pop) the plate that is **on the top**. - The plate placed **first** is at the **bottom** and will be accessed **last**. This is precisely the **LIFO discipline**. The most recent item added is the first one to leave. > [!NOTE] > **LIFO** is sometimes equivalently referred to as **FILO (First-In, Last-Out)**. Both denote the same access pattern but emphasize different temporal viewpoints. ### 1.3 Real-World Engineering Examples of Stacks - **Function call stack** in C++/Java runtime (stores return addresses, local variables, parameters). - **Undo/Redo** mechanism in text editors and IDEs. - **Browser back/forward** navigation history. - **Compiler recursion** — evaluation of recursive functions, DFS traversal. - **Memory management** — stack-allocated automatic variables. - **Backtracking algorithms** — maze solving, N-Queens, Sudoku. - **Expression evaluation** — infix, postfix, prefix conversions (covered in §2.3). ### 1.4 Importance in the KTU 2024 Syllabus > [!IMPORTANT] > Module 2 of OEITT612 places **stacks** before queues, trees, and graphs because stacks are foundational to: > 1. **Recursion implementation** in programming languages. > 2. **Compiler construction** (syntax analysis, expression evaluation). > 3. **Algorithm design** (DFS, backtracking, parenthesis matching). > Mastery of stack operations and their time complexities is **mandatory** for KTU ESE questions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stack as an Abstract Data Type (ADT) A stack is a **container ADT** characterized by a small, fixed set of operations. From an algebraic standpoint, we can formalize stack behavior using the following axioms: Let $S$ be a stack over a domain $D$ of elements. Define: - $\varepsilon$ as the empty stack. - $\text{top}(S)$ returns the most recently pushed element (undefined if $S = \varepsilon$). - $\text{push}(S, x) = S' = S \oplus x$ — appends $x$ to the top. - $\text{pop}(S) = S''$ — removes the top element. **Axiomatic Properties (KTU-Expected):** 1. $\text{top}(\text{push}(S, x)) = x$ — the top after pushing is the element just pushed. 2. $\text{pop}(\text{push}(S, x)) = S$ — push followed by pop restores the original stack. 3. $\text{isEmpty}(\varepsilon) = \text{true}$ — the empty stack is recognized. 4. $\text{isEmpty}(\text{push}(S, x)) = \text{false}$ — a non-empty stack after push. ### 2.2 Implementation Strategies — Two Paradigms The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates **two implementation paradigms** for stacks: #### 2.2.1 Array-Based Implementation (Static Stack) A contiguous block of memory of fixed maximum size `MAX` is allocated. A single integer index `top` tracks the position of the topmost element. **Memory Layout Diagram (Conceptual):** $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{index} & 0 & 1 & 2 & \cdots & \text{top}-1 & \text{top} & \cdots \\ \hline \text{content} & a_0 & a_1 & a_2 & \cdots & a_{\text{top}-1} & a_{\text{top}} & \text{unused} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ The cell at index `top` is the **topmost occupied slot**; the cell at index `top+1` is the **next free slot** for a push. **Overflow Condition (Array):** $$\text{Overflow} \iff \text{top} = \text{MAX} - 1$$ **Underflow Condition (Array):** $$\text{Underflow} \iff \text{top} = -1$$ #### 2.2.2 Linked-List-Based Implementation (Dynamic Stack) A **singly linked list** is used where the head pointer plays the role of the stack's `top`. Each node stores: - A `data` field (the element). - A `next` pointer to the node beneath it. Push inserts a new node at the head; pop removes the head node. There is **no fixed upper bound** (other than available heap memory). **Overflow Condition (Linked List):** $$\text{Overflow} \iff \text{Heap Memory Exhausted} \iff \text{new} \; \text{returns} \; \texttt{nullptr}$$ **Underflow Condition (Linked List):** $$\text{Underflow} \iff \text{top} = \texttt{nullptr} \;(\text{i.e., head is null})$$ ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU ESE numerical/short-answer questions on stacks. Memorize every row. | Property / Operation | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List-Based Stack | |----------------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Storage type | Static (compile-time) | Dynamic (runtime heap) | | Size limit | Fixed `MAX` | Grows/shrinks as needed | | Memory overhead | None (contiguous) | Pointer per node (8 bytes in 64-bit) | | `push()` time complexity | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | | `pop()` time complexity | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | | `peek()` time complexity | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | | `isEmpty()` check | `top == -1` | `top == nullptr` | | `isFull()` check | `top == MAX-1` | Always `false` (until OOM) | | Total space | $n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T) + \Theta(1)$ | $n \cdot (\text{sizeof}(T) + \text{sizeof}(ptr)) + \Theta(1)$ | | Cache locality | Excellent | Poor (scattered nodes) | | Resize possible? | Requires full reallocation | Just allocate new node | | Risk | Stack overflow (bounded) | Heap fragmentation / OOM | ### 2.4 Operator Precedence Table (For Infix/Postfix Applications) | Operator | Precedence | Associativity | |----------|------------|----------------| | `^` (exponentiation) | **3** (highest) | Right ($\rightarrow$) | | `*` , `/` , `%` | **2** | Left ($\leftarrow$) | | `+` , `-` | **1** (lowest) | Left ($\leftarrow$) | | `(` , `)` | Special (brackets) | N/A | The precedence ordering is **critical** for the infix-to-postfix conversion algorithm discussed in §3.4. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Stack Use-Case | |--------------------|----------------| | Compilers | Syntax parsing, recursive-descent parsing, intermediate code generation | | Operating Systems | Process call stacks, context switching, interrupt handling | | Embedded Systems | Return-address stack in microcontrollers, task scheduling | | Networking | Routing protocol backtracking, packet reassembly | | Computer Graphics | Flood-fill algorithm, undo operations in drawing tools | | Database Systems | Transaction log, recovery using redo/undo logs | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Algorithms & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 C++ Implementation — Array-Based Stack **Complete C++ Program (KTU Board-Expected Style):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; const int MAX = 100; class Stack { private: int arr[MAX]; int top; public: Stack() { top = -1; } // PUSH: Insert element x at the top of the stack void push(int x) { if (isFull()) { cout << "STACK OVERFLOW! Cannot push " << x << endl; return; } arr[++top] = x; // Step 1: increment top, then store cout << x << " pushed." << endl; } // POP: Remove and return the top element int pop() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "STACK UNDERFLOW! Nothing to pop." << endl; return -1; } return arr[top--]; // Step 1: return, then decrement top } // PEEK: Return top element without removing int peek() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "STACK IS EMPTY." << endl; return -1; } return arr[top]; } bool isEmpty() { return top == -1; } bool isFull() { return top == MAX - 1; } void display() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "[empty]" << endl; return; } cout << "Stack (top -> bottom): "; for (int i = top; i >= 0; i--) cout << arr[i] << " "; cout << endl; } }; int main() { Stack s; s.push(10); s.push(20); s.push(30); s.display(); cout << "Top element: " << s.peek() << endl; cout << "Popped: " << s.pop() << endl; s.display(); return 0; } ``` **Expected Output Trace:** ``` 10 pushed. 20 pushed. 30 pushed. Stack (top -> bottom): 30 20 10 Top element: 30 Popped: 30 Stack (top -> bottom): 20 10 ``` **Algorithmic Complexity Derivation (PUSH):** The push operation performs three constant-time actions: 1. **Check overflow**: one comparison → $O(1)$ 2. **Increment `top`**: one arithmetic op → $O(1)$ 3. **Assign `arr[top] = x`**: one memory write → $O(1)$ $$ T_{\text{push}}(n) = O(1) + O(1) + O(1) = \Theta(1) $$ Similarly for `pop`, `peek`, `isEmpty`, `isFull`: each is a single comparison and/or arithmetic operation, hence all $\Theta(1)$. ### 3.2 C++ Implementation — Linked-List-Based Stack **Complete C++ Program (KTU Board-Expected Style):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; // Node structure struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int v) : data(v), next(nullptr) {} }; class LinkedStack { private: Node* topNode; // Head of the linked list public: LinkedStack() { topNode = nullptr; } // PUSH: Insert new node at the head void push(int x) { Node* newNode = new Node(x); // Allocate on heap if (newNode == nullptr) { cout << "HEAP OVERFLOW! Cannot push " << x << endl; return; } newNode->next = topNode; // Link to old top topNode = newNode; // Update top cout << x << " pushed." << endl; } // POP: Remove head node int pop() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "STACK UNDERFLOW!" << endl; return -1; } Node* temp = topNode; int val = temp->data; topNode = topNode->next; // Move top to next delete temp; // Free memory return val; } // PEEK: Return top element's data int peek() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "STACK IS EMPTY." << endl; return -1; } return topNode->data; } bool isEmpty() { return topNode == nullptr; } void display() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "[empty]" << endl; return; } cout << "Stack (top -> bottom): "; Node* cur = topNode; while (cur != nullptr) { cout << cur->data << " "; cur = cur->next; } cout << endl; } // Destructor to free all nodes ~LinkedStack() { while (!isEmpty()) pop(); } }; int main() { LinkedStack s; s.push(100); s.push(200); s.push(300); s.display(); cout << "Top: " << s.peek() << endl; cout << "Popped: " << s.pop() << endl; s.display(); return 0; } ``` **Expected Output Trace:** ``` 100 pushed. 200 pushed. 300 pushed. Stack (top -> bottom): 300 200 100 Top: 300 Popped: 300 Stack (top -> bottom): 200 100 ``` **Algorithmic Complexity Derivation (PUSH — Linked):** 1. **Heap allocation** (`new Node(x)`): $O(1)$ amortized 2. **Link update** (`newNode->next = topNode`): $O(1)$ 3. **Top update** (`topNode = newNode`): $O(1)$ $$ T_{\text{push,linked}}(n) = \Theta(1) $$ **Memory Footprint Comparison:** For $n$ elements of type `int` (4 bytes) on a 64-bit system (8-byte pointers): $$ S_{\text{array}}(n) = 4n + 4 \text{ bytes} \quad (\text{plus padding to align}) $$ $$ S_{\text{linked}}(n) = (4 + 8) \cdot n + 8 = 12n + 8 \text{ bytes} $$ The linked-list version consumes **3× more memory** per element due to pointer overhead. ### 3.3 Application 1 — Balanced Parentheses Checker **Problem Statement:** Given an expression containing `()`, `{}`, `[]`, determine if all brackets are correctly matched and nested. **Algorithm (KTU Expected Pseudocode):** ``` ALGORITHM isBalanced(expr): create an empty stack S for each character c in expr (left to right): if c is an opening bracket '(', '{', '[': S.push(c) else if c is a closing bracket ')', '}', ']': if S.isEmpty() return FALSE top = S.pop() if top does not match c (by type): return FALSE return S.isEmpty() ``` **Matching Table:** | Opening | Closing | |---------|---------| | `(` | `)` | | `{` | `}` | | `[` | `]` | **Trace Example:** Input: `{[()]}` $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Char} & \textbf{Action} & \textbf{Stack (top→bottom)} & \textbf{Result} \\ \hline 1 & \texttt{\{} & push & \texttt{\{} & OK \\ 2 & \texttt{[} & push & \texttt{[}\;\texttt{\{} & OK \\ 3 & \texttt{(} & push & \texttt{(}\;\texttt{[}\;\texttt{\{} & OK \\ 4 & \texttt{)} & pop \& match & \texttt{[}\;\texttt{\{} & Match with `(' \\ 5 & \texttt{]} & pop \& match & \texttt{\{} & Match with `[' \\ 6 & \texttt{\}} & pop \& match & \texttt{(empty)} & Match with `\{' \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Final Result:** Stack is empty → **Balanced** ✓ **Time Complexity:** Each character is pushed/popped at most once. $$ T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{where } n = \text{length of expression} $$ **Space Complexity (Worst Case):** All opening brackets: $$ S(n) = O(n) $$ ### 3.4 Application 2 — Infix to Postfix Conversion (Shunting-Yard Algorithm) **Problem Statement:** Convert an infix expression (e.g., `A + B * C`) into a postfix expression (e.g., `A B C * +`) using a stack. **Algorithm (KTU Expected — Dijkstra's Shunting-Yard):** ``` ALGORITHM infixToPostfix(infix): create empty stack S (for operators) create empty string postfix for each token t in infix (left to right): if t is an OPERAND: append t to postfix else if t == '(': S.push(t) else if t == ')': while S.peek() != '(': append S.pop() to postfix S.pop() // discard the '(' else if t is an OPERATOR: while (not S.isEmpty()) AND (precedence(t) < precedence(S.peek()) OR (precedence(t) == precedence(S.peek()) AND isLeftAssoc(t))): append S.pop() to postfix S.push(t) while not S.isEmpty(): append S.pop() to postfix return postfix ``` **Trace Example:** Infix: `A + B * C - D / E` $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Token} & \textbf{Action} & \textbf{Stack} & \textbf{Postfix} \\ \hline 1 & A & operand & (empty) & A \\ 2 & + & push & + & A \\ 3 & B & operand & + & AB \\ 4 & * & prec(*) > prec(+), push & +* & AB \\ 5 & C & operand & +* & ABC \\ 6 & - & prec(-) < prec(*), pop * & + & ABC* \\ & & prec(-) = prec(+), left-assoc, pop + & (empty) & ABC*+ \\ & & push - & - & ABC*+ \\ 7 & D & operand & - & ABC*+D \\ 8 & / & prec(/) > prec(-), push & -/ & ABC*+D \\ 9 & E & operand & -/ & ABC*+DE \\ 10 & END & pop /, pop - & (empty) & ABC*+DE/- \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Result:** `ABC*+DE/-` **Time Complexity:** $$ T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{since each token is pushed and popped at most once} $$ **Space Complexity (Worst Case):** All operators: $$ S(n) = O(n) $$ ### 3.5 Application 3 — Postfix Expression Evaluation **Problem Statement:** Evaluate a postfix expression using a stack of operands. **Algorithm (KTU Expected):** ``` ALGORITHM evaluatePostfix(postfix): create empty stack S (for operands) for each token t in postfix (left to right): if t is an OPERAND: S.push(value(t)) else if t is an OPERATOR: operand2 = S.pop() // right operand operand1 = S.pop() // left operand result = apply(t, operand1, operand2) S.push(result) return S.pop() ``` **Crucial Point:** For non-commutative operators (`-`, `/`, `^`), the **first pop is the right operand** and the **second pop is the left operand**. **Trace Example:** Postfix: `5 6 2 + * 12 4 / -` $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Token} & \textbf{Action} & \textbf{Stack} \\ \hline 1 & 5 & push 5 & 5 \\ 2 & 6 & push 6 & 5\;6 \\ 3 & 2 & push 2 & 5\;6\;2 \\ 4 & + & pop 2,6 → 6+2=8, push 8 & 5\;8 \\ 5 & * & pop 8,5 → 5*8=40, push 40 & 40 \\ 6 & 12 & push 12 & 40\;12 \\ 7 & 4 & push 4 & 40\;12\;4 \\ 8 & / & pop 4,12 → 12/4=3, push 3 & 40\;3 \\ 9 & - & pop 3,40 → 40-3=37, push 37 & 37 \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Final Result:** `37` **Verification (Infix form):** $((5 \times (6+2)) - (12/4)) = (5 \times 8) - 3 = 40 - 3 = 37$ ✓ **Time Complexity:** $$ T(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(each token is processed exactly once)} $$ ### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Both Implementations | Criterion | Array Stack | Linked-List Stack | Verdict | |-----------|-------------|-------------------|---------| | Memory allocation | Static | Dynamic | Linked is more flexible | | Memory utilization | May waste space if not full | Uses exactly what is needed | Linked is more efficient | | Access time (push/pop) | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | **Tie** | | Cache performance | High (contiguous) | Low (scattered) | Array wins | | Resizing | Requires full reallocation (costly) | Just `new` a node | Linked wins | | Extra memory per element | 0 bytes | 8 bytes (pointer) | Array wins | | Code complexity | Simple | Moderate (pointer manipulation) | Array wins | | Memory leaks | None | Possible (needs destructor) | Array wins | | Predictable performance | Yes | Subject to allocator behavior | Array wins | ### 3.7 Stack Overflow / Underflow — Formal Definitions $$ \text{Overflow}_{\text{array}} \iff \text{top} \geq \text{MAX} - 1 $$ $$ \text{Overflow}_{\text{linked}} \iff \text{new} \; \text{fails} \iff \text{available heap} = 0 $$ $$ \text{Underflow}_{\text{any}} \iff \text{top} \; \text{is at the initial empty marker} \; (\text{-1 or nullptr}) $$ > [!NOTE] > Underflow is a **logic error** (you tried to pop from an empty stack), whereas overflow in array implementation is a **capacity error** (you tried to push beyond the array's allocated size). Both must be explicitly checked in production-grade C++ code. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Array-Based Stack After Three Pushes ```mermaid graph LR A0["arr[0] = 10"] A1["arr[1] = 20"] A2["arr[2] = 30
TOP"] A3["arr[3] = UNUSED"] A4["arr[4] = UNUSED"] TD["arr[MAX-1] = UNUSED"] TOP["top = 2"] A0 --- A1 A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 A3 --- A4 A4 --- TD A2 -. points to .-> TOP ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Linked-List-Based Stack After Three Pushes ```mermaid graph LR TOP["TOP pointer"] N1["Node1
data=30
next=Node2"] N2["Node2
data=20
next=Node3"] N3["Node3
data=10
next=nullptr"] TOP --> N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — PUSH Operation on Linked Stack (Inserting 40) ```mermaid graph LR OLDTOP["Old TOP"] N1["Node1
data=30"] N2["Node2
data=20"] N3["Node3
data=10
next=null"] NEWTOP["New TOP"] NEWN["NewNode
data=40"] OLDTOP -. before .-> N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 NEWTOP --> NEWN NEWN -. next points to .-> N1 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — POP Operation on Linked Stack (Removing 30) ```mermaid graph LR OLDBEFORE["Before POP"] OLDTOP["TOP"] N1["Node1
data=30
TO BE DELETED"] N2["Node2
data=20"] N3["Node3
data=10
next=null"] NEWTOP2["After POP"] NEWTOPS["TOP (updated)"] OLDBEFORE --> OLDTOP OLDTOP --> N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 NEWTOP2 --> NEWTOPS NEWTOPS --> N2 N1 -. delete .-> X["x"] ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Infix to Postfix Conversion Flow ```mermaid graph TD START["Start: Read Infix Token"] OP{"Is token
an operand?"} OPSTK["Append to output string"] POPOP["Pop operators
with higher/equal
precedence"] PUSHOP["Push current
operator to stack"] LPAREN["Push '(' to stack"] RPAREN["Pop until '(' found"] ENDEXP["End of expression"] DUMPSTK["Pop all remaining
operators"] RESULT["Final Postfix String"] START --> OP OP -- Yes --> OPSTK OP -- No --> OP2{"Is it '('?"} OP2 -- Yes --> LPAREN OP2 -- No --> OP3{"Is it ')'?"} OP3 -- Yes --> RPAREN OP3 -- No --> OP4{"Is it
operator?"} OP4 -- Yes --> POPOP POPOP --> PUSHOP OP4 -- No --> NEXT["Next token"] OPSTK --> NEXT LPAREN --> NEXT RPAREN --> NEXT PUSHOP --> NEXT NEXT --> START ENDEXP --> DUMPSTK DUMPSTK --> RESULT ``` ### 4.6 Mermaid Diagram — Postfix Evaluation Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["Read Postfix Token"] B{"Is token
an operand?"} C["Push operand
to stack"] D{"Is token
an operator?"} E["Pop right operand
b = S.pop()"] F["Pop left operand
a = S.pop()"] G["Compute result = a OP b"] H["Push result to stack"] I["End of tokens"] J["Return S.pop()
Final Result"] A --> B B -- Yes --> C B -- No --> D D -- Yes --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> K["Next token"] D -- No --> ERROR["Error: Invalid token"] C --> K K --> A I --> J ``` ### 4.7 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Stack ADT Internals ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CLIENT["Client Application Layer"] UI["User Interface / main()"] end subgraph ADT["Stack Abstract Data Type"] PUSHOP["push(x)"] POPOP["pop()"] PEEKOP["peek()"] EMPTYCHK["isEmpty()"] FULLCHK["isFull()"] DISPOP["display()"] end subgraph IMPL["Implementation Layer"] ARRAYSUB["Array-Based Class"] LINKSUB["Linked-List-Based Class"] end subgraph DATA["Storage Layer"] ARR["Static int arr[MAX]"] NODES["Heap-allocated Node* chain"] end UI --> PUSHOP UI --> POPOP UI --> PEEKOP UI --> EMPTYCHK UI --> FULLCHK UI --> DISPOP PUSHOP --> ARRAYSUB PUSHOP --> LINKSUB POPOP --> ARRAYSUB POPOP --> LINKSUB ARRAYSUB --> ARR LINKSUB --> NODES ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** *Define a stack. Explain the LIFO principle with a real-world example. List any two applications of stacks.* **Model Answer (3 marks — Awarded Component-Wise):** A **stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** discipline, where elements are inserted and removed from the same end, called the **TOP**. **LIFO Principle (1 mark):** The element inserted most recently is the one removed first. Only the topmost element is accessible at any time. **Real-World Example (1 mark):** A pile of plates in a cafeteria — you can only add or remove the topmost plate; the plate placed first will be taken out last. **Two Applications (1 mark):** 1. **Function call stack** in C++/Java for managing return addresses during recursive calls. 2. **Undo operation** in text editors — every action is pushed onto a stack; undo pops the most recent action. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** *Differentiate between stack implementation using array and stack implementation using linked list. Mention the time complexity of push and pop in each case.* **Model Answer (3 marks — Tabular Format Expected):** | Parameter | Array-Based Stack | Linked-List Stack | |-----------|-------------------|-------------------| | Size | Fixed (MAX) | Dynamic | | Memory | Contiguous, static | Scattered, heap | | Push time | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | | Pop time | $\Theta(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | | Overflow check | Required (`top == MAX-1`) | Not needed (until OOM) | | Extra memory | None | One pointer per node | **Key Insight (1 mark):** Both implementations yield $\Theta(1)$ push/pop, but the array version has better cache locality, while the linked version allows unbounded growth. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **QUESTION A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style, Module 2]** **(a) [7 marks]** Implement a stack using an array in C++. Write member functions for `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()`, `isEmpty()`, and `isFull()`. Show the state of the stack after the following sequence of operations: `push(5), push(10), push(15), pop(), peek(), push(20)` **(b) [7 marks]** Explain the algorithm to check whether a given expression has balanced parentheses using a stack. Trace the algorithm for the expression `{[( )]}` and `{[}]`. **Model Solution for (a):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; const int MAX = 100; class Stack { int arr[MAX]; int top; public: Stack() { top = -1; } bool isEmpty() { return top == -1; } bool isFull() { return top == MAX - 1; } void push(int x) { if (isFull()) { cout << "Overflow\n"; return; } arr[++top] = x; } int pop() { if (isEmpty()) { cout << "Underflow\n"; return -1; } return arr[top--]; } int peek() { if (isEmpty()) return -1; return arr[top]; } void display() { for (int i = top; i >= 0; i--) cout << arr[i] << " "; cout << endl; } }; int main() { Stack s; s.push(5); s.display(); s.push(10); s.display(); s.push(15); s.display(); cout << "Popped: " << s.pop() << endl; s.display(); cout << "Top: " << s.peek() << endl; s.push(20); s.display(); return 0; } ``` **Trace Table (1 mark awarded for correct trace):** $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Operation} & \textbf{top value} & \textbf{Stack (top→bottom)} \\ \hline 1 & \texttt{push(5)} & 0 & 5 \\ 2 & \texttt{push(10)} & 1 & 10\;5 \\ 3 & \texttt{push(15)} & 2 & 15\;10\;5 \\ 4 & \texttt{pop()} \to \text{returns 15} & 1 & 10\;5 \\ 5 & \texttt{peek()} \to \text{returns 10} & 1 & 10\;5 \\ 6 & \texttt{push(20)} & 2 & 20\;10\;5 \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Model Solution for (b):** **Algorithm (3 marks):** ``` 1. Create empty stack S 2. For each character c in the expression: a. If c is opening bracket (', '{', '['): S.push(c) b. If c is closing bracket (')', '}', ']'): i. If S is empty → UNBALANCED ii. Pop top element; if it doesn't match c → UNBALANCED 3. After scan: if S is empty → BALANCED, else UNBALANCED ``` **Trace for `{[( )]}` (2 marks):** $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Char} & \textbf{Stack} & \textbf{Status} \\ \hline 1 & \texttt{\{} & \texttt{\{} & OK \\ 2 & \texttt{(} & \texttt{\{\{} & (mistype in source) actually \{ ( \quad → OK \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Corrected Trace (2 marks):** | Step | Char | Stack (top→bottom) | Action | |------|------|---------------------|--------| | 1 | `{` | `{` | push | | 2 | `[` | `[ {` | push | | 3 | `(` | `( [ {` | push | | 4 | `)` | `[ {` | pop, matches `(` | | 5 | `]` | `{` | pop, matches `[` | | 6 | `}` | (empty) | pop, matches `{` | **Result:** Stack empty → **Balanced** ✓ **Trace for `{[}]` (1 mark):** | Step | Char | Stack | Action | |------|------|-------|--------| | 1 | `{` | `{` | push | | 2 | `[` | `[ {` | push | | 3 | `}` | (empty after pop) | Pop `[`, but `}` ≠ `[` → **MISMATCH** | **Result:** **Not Balanced** ✗ --- **QUESTION B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style, Module 2]** **(a) [7 marks]** Implement a stack using a **linked list** in C++. Write the class definition with `push()`, `pop()`, `peek()`, and `isEmpty()` operations. Explain how memory is managed on the heap. **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the **infix-to-postfix conversion** algorithm using a stack. Convert the infix expression `A + (B * C - D) / E` to postfix, showing the stack state at every step. **Model Solution for (a):** ```cpp #include using namespace std; struct Node { int data; Node* next; Node(int v) : data(v), next(nullptr) {} }; class Stack { Node* top; public: Stack() : top(nullptr) {} bool isEmpty() { return top == nullptr; } void push(int x) { Node* n = new Node(x); n->next = top; top = n; } int pop() { if (isEmpty()) return -1; Node* temp = top; int val = temp->data; top = top->next; delete temp; return val; } int peek() { if (isEmpty()) return -1; return top->data; } ~Stack() { while (!isEmpty()) pop(); } }; ``` **Memory Management Explanation (2 marks):** - Each `push()` calls `new Node(x)` to allocate memory on the **heap** (dynamic storage). - Each `pop()` calls `delete temp` to **deallocate** the removed node, preventing memory leaks. - The destructor (`~Stack()`) iterates and pops until empty, ensuring **no memory leak** when the stack object goes out of scope. - The linked list is **non-contiguous** — nodes may reside at arbitrary heap addresses. **Model Solution for (b):** **Algorithm Summary (2 marks):** See §3.4 for the complete pseudocode. **Precedence Used:** `*`, `/` > `+`, `-` (left associative); `(` and `)` are delimiters. **Trace for `A + (B * C - D) / E` (5 marks):** $$ \begin{array}{|c|l|c|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Step} & \textbf{Token} & \textbf{Action} & \textbf{Stack} & \textbf{Postfix Output} \\ \hline 1 & A & output & (empty) & A \\ 2 & + & push & + & A \\ 3 & ( & push & ( + & A \\ 4 & B & output & ( + & AB \\ 5 & * & push & * ( + & AB \\ 6 & C & output & * ( + & ABC \\ 7 & - & prec(-) < prec(*), pop * & ( + & ABC* \\ & & push - & - ( + & ABC* \\ 8 & D & output & - ( + & ABC*D \\ 9 & ) & pop until ( & + & ABC*D- \\ & & discard ( & + & ABC*D- \\ 10 & / & prec(/) = prec(+), left-assoc, pop + & (empty) & ABC*D-+ \\ & & push / & / & ABC*D-+ \\ 11 & E & output & / & ABC*D-+E \\ 12 & END & pop / & (empty) & ABC*D-+E/ \\ \hline \end{array} $$ **Final Postfix Expression:** `ABC*D-+E/` **Verification (optional bonus mark):** - Infix tree: `+` is root, left=`A`, right=`/`, with right-left=`-` and right-right=`E` - Post-order traversal: `A ((B*C) - D) / E` → `ABC*D-+E/` ✓ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Forgetting to check overflow/underflow** — Examiners explicitly look for these checks. Failure to include them typically results in a **2-mark deduction**. > 2. **Wrong operand order in postfix evaluation** — The **first popped** value is the **right operand**, the **second popped** is the **left operand**. Reversing this yields wrong answers (e.g., `5 - 2` becomes `2 - 5`). > 3. **Confusing precedence with associativity** — In infix-to-postfix, equal-precedence operators are popped only if the current operator is **left-associative**. For right-associative operators (like `^`), equal-precedence operators are **not** popped. > 4. **Forgetting to empty the stack at the end** of infix-to-postfix conversion — leftover operators in the stack at the end of the scan are appended to the postfix output. > 5. **Not handling memory leak** in linked-list stack — Always include a destructor that pops all remaining nodes. > 6. **Mixing up `top++` vs `++top`** — `arr[top++]` in pop is **wrong**; it returns the element and then increments, but we need to **decrement after returning** the topmost element. Correct: `return arr[top--]`. > 7. **Using `nullptr` checks incorrectly** — In a linked stack, `top == nullptr` means empty; do not dereference it without checking. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Stacks** - ☐ **Stack** = LIFO linear data structure with one accessible end (TOP). - ☐ Two core operations: **PUSH** (insert) and **POP** (delete), both $\Theta(1)$. - ☐ **Auxiliary operations:** `peek()`, `isEmpty()`, `isFull()`. - ☐ **Array implementation:** static, bounded, contiguous, fast cache access. - Overflow: `top == MAX - 1` - Underflow: `top == -1` - ☐ **Linked-list implementation:** dynamic, unbounded, scattered in heap, needs pointer management. - Overflow: heap exhaustion - Underflow: `top == nullptr` - ☐ **Both implementations** have $\Theta(1)$ push/pop, but linked list has $O(n)$ extra pointer overhead. - ☐ **Balanced parentheses algorithm:** push opening, pop and match on closing; final empty stack = balanced. Time: $O(n)$, Space: $O(n)$. - ☐ **Infix-to-postfix algorithm (Shunting-Yard):** output operands directly; use stack to hold operators; pop higher-or-equal precedence operators before pushing the current one. Time: $O(n)$, Space: $O(n)$. - ☐ **Postfix evaluation algorithm:** push operands, pop two for each operator, apply, push result. Time: $O(n)$, Space: $O(n)$. - ☐ **Order of pops in postfix evaluation:** first pop = right operand, second pop = left operand (critical for `-`, `/`, `^`). - ☐ **Destructor is mandatory** in linked-list stack class to prevent memory leaks. - ☐ **Standard precedence:** `^` > `* / %` > `+ -`; `^` is right-associative, others are left-associative. - ☐ **Real-world uses:** function call stack, undo/redo, browser history, backtracking, DFS, expression parsing. - ☐ **Memory sizes:** Array stack = $n \cdot \text{sizeof}(T)$; Linked stack = $n \cdot (\text{sizeof}(T) + \text{sizeof}(ptr))$. > [!NOTE] > **Recommended Self-Study Exercise (Not for Exam, but for Mastery):** > Implement a stack that supports a `getMin()` operation in $O(1)$ time, returning the minimum element currently in the stack. This is a classic interview problem and a KTU-favorite challenging question. (Hint: use an auxiliary stack to track minima.) ---

topics.title: Queues: Implementation Using Arrays and Linked Lists, Circular Queue, Priority Queue
C++ STL Implementation of Linear Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** C++ STL Implementation of Linear Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **C++ Standard Template Library (STL)** is a powerful, generic, compile-time-polymorphic software library that forms a fundamental part of the ISO C++ Standard Library. It provides ready-to-use, highly efficient, and reusable implementations of common data structures (called **containers**) and algorithms (called **algorithms**) that operate on them through generalized interfaces using **iterators** and **function objects (functors)**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Linear Data Structures** refer specifically to **sequence containers** in the STL — containers whose elements are arranged in a strict linear sequence, and each element is accessible based on its position in that sequence. The four principal STL sequence containers covered in this module are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus-Mandated Sequence Containers (Module 2)**
> 1. **`std::vector<T>`** — Dynamic contiguous array
> 2. **`std::list<T>`** — Doubly linked list
> 3. **`std::deque<T>`** — Double-ended queue (segmented array)
> 4. **`std::array<T, N>`** — Fixed-size contiguous array (C++11 onwards)
> 5. **`std::forward_list<T>`** — Singly linked list (briefly mentioned)

Each container belongs to the namespace `std` and is declared in its own header file (e.g., `<vector>`, `<list>`, `<deque>`, `<array>`, `<forward_list>`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the STL as a **fully-stocked, modular industrial kitchen**:

| Kitchen Element | STL Equivalent | Real Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerators, shelves, racks | **Containers** | Storage where data actually lives |
| The cook's hands that reach in | **Iterators** | Generic pointers to access/cook the ingredients |
| Recipe book (sort, search, etc.) | **Algorithms** | Pre-written procedures to manipulate data |
| Recipe adjustments (less salt, more spice) | **Functors / Lambdas** | Customization knobs for the recipes |
| The chef's apprenticeship | **Templates `<T>`** | A single recipe works for *any* ingredient type |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why STL matters for KTU exams:** The examiner expects you to know not just the *syntax* of `push_back()`, `insert()`, `erase()`, etc., but the **internal mechanism** (contiguous vs. linked storage), the **time complexity** of each operation, and **when to choose which container** — these are the most frequently tested concepts in Module 2.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Expected Values)

All complexities are expressed in **Big-O notation** $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$ and are considered **amortized** where noted. These are the values you **must memorize** verbatim for the KTU exam:

- **Contiguous random access** (vector, deque, array): $\mathcal{O}(1)$
- **Linked list traversal step** (list, forward_list): $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per node hop
- **Amortized append** (vector `push_back`): $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized
- **Linear search** (unsorted sequence): $\mathcal{O}(n)$
- **Binary search** (sorted random-access sequence): $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of the STL

The STL is built upon four tightly-integrated components. Understanding the role of each is **mandatory** for Module 2:

1. **Containers** — Class templates that hold collections of objects.
2. **Iterators** — Objects that act like generalized pointers, providing a uniform interface to traverse containers.
3. **Algorithms** — Function templates that perform operations (sort, find, count, etc.) on ranges defined by iterators.
4. **Function Objects (Functors)** — Objects that overload `operator()` to provide custom comparison/predicates.

## 2.2 Internal Architecture of Each Sequence Container

### 2.2.1 `std::vector<T>` — Dynamic Contiguous Array
- Backed by a single contiguous block of dynamically allocated memory (on the heap, typically).
- Maintains three internal pointers: `begin`, `end`, and `capacity_end`.
- **Geometric reallocation strategy:** when `size() == capacity()`, the vector allocates a new block of size $2 \times$ (or $1.5\times$, implementation-defined) the old capacity, copies/moves all elements, and deallocates the old block.

### 2.2.2 `std::list<T>` — Doubly Linked List
- Each node stores the element, a pointer to the next node, and a pointer to the previous node.
- Provides **constant-time insertion and deletion at any iterator position**, but **no random access** — accessing the $k$-th element requires $\mathcal{O}(k)$ traversal.
- Uses an internal sentinel (dummy) node to make `end()`'s implementation uniform.

### 2.2.3 `std::deque<T>` — Double-Ended Queue
- Internally implemented as a **segmented array of fixed-size blocks** (often arrays of pointers, each pointing to a smaller array of elements).
- Allows **constant-time insertion/deletion at both the front and the back**, and **random access** in $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
- Does **not** invalidate iterators on `push_back` / `push_front` (unlike vector), but does invalidate them on insertion in the middle.

### 2.2.4 `std::array<T, N>` — Fixed-Size Contiguous Array
- A thin wrapper over a C-style array of exactly $N$ elements.
- **Zero overhead** — no dynamic allocation, no extra pointers.
- Size must be known at compile time.

### 2.2.5 `std::forward_list<T>` — Singly Linked List
- Each node has only a `next` pointer → smaller memory footprint than `std::list`.
- Supports `insert_after`, `erase_after`, `before_begin` — note the asymmetric API.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU Module 2. Reproduce it from memory before every exam.

**Table 2.1 — Time Complexity of Sequence Container Operations**

| Operation | `vector` | `deque` | `list` | `forward_list` | `array` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Random Access `v[i]` | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Insert/Delete at front | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Not possible |
| Insert/Delete at back | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amort. | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Not possible |
| Insert/Delete at middle | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ at iter. | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ at iter. after | Not possible |
| `size()` | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Iterator stability on realloc | Invalidated | Most stable | Fully stable | Fully stable | N/A (no realloc) |
| Memory layout | Contiguous | Segmented contiguous | Heap nodes | Heap nodes | Contiguous stack/heap |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pet Trick:** When asked "Why is `std::list` *not* always faster than `std::vector`?" — the answer lies in **cache locality**. A contiguous `vector` exploits CPU cache lines, so a tight loop over a vector is often *faster* than the equivalent loop over a `list`, despite the asymptotic advantage of `list` insertions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **`vector`** is the default workhorse — used in graphics (vertex buffers), numerical simulations, and dynamic arrays in OS kernels.
- **`list`** is used in LRU caches, undo-redo stacks, and any algorithm requiring stable iterators.
- **`deque`** is the default underlying container for `std::queue` and `std::stack` in many implementations and is used in work-stealing schedulers.
- **`array`** is preferred over C-style arrays in embedded/safety-critical code for type safety and STL algorithm compatibility.
- **`forward_list`** is used in memory-constrained environments (e.g., kernel data structures, graph adjacency lists).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Amortized Cost of `vector::push_back`

This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** ("Explain why `push_back` on a vector is called $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized, with proof"). We derive it here in full.

**Assumption:** On reallocation, the new capacity $C_{\text{new}}$ is $2 \times C_{\text{old}}$ (geometric growth).

**Logic Step 1 — Count total copies.** Suppose we grow from capacity $1$ to capacity $n$ by repeatedly calling `push_back`. Let the capacities be $1, 2, 4, 8, \dots, n$, where $n$ is a power of $2$. The number of reallocations is $\log_2 n$.

**Logic Step 2 — Sum the work.** When the vector grows from capacity $2^{k-1}$ to $2^{k}$, it must copy all $2^{k-1}$ existing elements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{k=1}^{\log_2 n} 2^{k-1} \\
     &= 2^{0} + 2^{1} + 2^{2} + \dots + 2^{\log_2 n - 1} \\
     &= 2^{\log_2 n} - 1 \\
     &= n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Step 3 — Apply the amortized definition.** The amortized cost per push is the total work divided by the number of operations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Amortized cost} &= \frac{T(n)}{n} = \frac{n - 1}{n} = 1 - \frac{1}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic Step 4 — Take the limit / bound.** As $n \to \infty$, the amortized cost per `push_back` is bounded by a constant:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{n} \right) = 1
$$

Therefore, by definition of Big-O for amortized analysis, `push_back` is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized. The "worst case" of a single `push_back` is $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (during reallocation), but this happens so rarely that the average over a sequence of $n$ operations is constant.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Iterator Invalidation Rules

The KTU 2024 module frequently asks: *"After performing operation X, which iterators become invalid?"* Memorize the matrix below.

**Table 3.1 — Iterator Invalidation Matrix**

| Container | Operation | Invalidated Iterators |
|---|---|---|
| `vector` | `insert` / `erase` | All iterators at or after the point of change |
| `vector` | `push_back` (no realloc) | `end()` only |
| `vector` | `push_back` (with realloc) | **All** iterators |
| `deque` | `insert` / `erase` at middle | **All** iterators |
| `deque` | `push_back` / `push_front` | Iterators, but **not** references to elements |
| `list` | `insert` / `erase` / `push_back` / `push_front` | Only the erased iterator |
| `forward_list` | `insert_after` / `erase_after` | Only iterators to the erased element |
| `array` | Any operation | N/A (size is constant) |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Vector Capacity Growth Trace

**Problem (KTU Style):** A `vector<int>` starts empty. Show the state of `size()`, `capacity()`, and the underlying memory addresses after each of these operations: `push_back(10)`, `push_back(20)`, `push_back(30)`, `push_back(40)`, `pop_back()`, `push_back(50)`.

**Step-by-Step Trace (assuming doubling growth):**

| Step | Operation | Size | Capacity | Elements | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | 0 | 0 | — | `nullptr` |
| 1 | `push_back(10)` | 1 | 1 | 10 | `0x100` |
| 2 | `push_back(20)` | 2 | 2 | 10, 20 | `0x200` |
| 3 | `push_back(30)` | 3 | 4 | 10, 20, 30 | `0x300` |
| 4 | `push_back(40)` | 4 | 4 | 10, 20, 30, 40 | `0x300` |
| 5 | `pop_back()` | 3 | 4 | 10, 20, 30 | `0x300` |
| 6 | `push_back(50)` | 4 | 4 | 10, 20, 30, 50 | `0x300` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** `pop_back()` only decrements `size`. It does **not** reduce `capacity`. To shrink, one must use the `swap-with-empty` idiom: `vector<int>(v).swap(v);` or, in C++11+, the `shrink_to_fit()` request.

## 3.4 Worked Example: `std::list` vs `std::vector` Insertion

**Problem:** Insert the value `99` at the **3rd position** in a container holding $\{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$.

**For `vector`:** The container must shift elements at indices $\geq 2$ one position to the right. Number of element moves:

$$
\text{Moves} = n - k = 5 - 2 = 3 \quad \text{elements}
$$

Time complexity: $\mathcal{O}(n)$.

**For `list`:** The container must first traverse to the 3rd node (cost: 2 pointer hops = $\mathcal{O}(k)$), allocate a new node ($\mathcal{O}(1)$), and rewire 4 pointers ($\mathcal{O}(1)$).

$$
\text{Time} = \mathcal{O}(k) + \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(k)
$$

For $k = 2$, this is effectively constant — but for a `list` of 1 million elements inserting at position 500,000, the traversal itself is $\mathcal{O}(500{,}000)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 STL Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[STL Components] --> B[Containers]
    A --> C[Iterators]
    A --> D[Algorithms]
    A --> E[Functors and Function Objects]
    B --> B1[Sequence Containers]
    B --> B2[Associative Containers]
    B --> B3[Unordered Associative]
    B1 --> S1[vector]
    B1 --> S2[deque]
    B1 --> S3[list]
    B1 --> S4[forward_list]
    B1 --> S5[array]
    C --> I1[Input Iterator]
    C --> I2[Forward Iterator]
    C --> I3[Bidirectional Iterator]
    C --> I4[Random Access Iterator]
    D --> ALG1[Non Modifying: find, count, search]
    D --> ALG2[Modifying: copy, transform, fill]
    D --> ALG3[Sorting: sort, stable_sort, partial_sort]
    D --> ALG4[Heap: make_heap, push_heap, pop_heap]
    E --> F1[Predicates]
    E --> F2[Arithmetic Functors]
    E --> F3[Lambda Expressions]
```

## 4.2 Internal Memory Layout: `std::vector`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAP[Heap Allocated Contiguous Block]
    N0[Index 0] --> N1[Index 1]
    N1 --> N2[Index 2]
    N2 --> N3[Index 3]
    N3 --> N4[Index 4]
    N4 --> N5[Index 5]
    N5 --> N6[Index 6 - capacity end]
    N6 --> FREE[Unallocated Memory]
    BEG[beg pointer] -.-> N0
    END[end pointer] -.-> N3
    CAP[capacity end pointer] -.-> N6
```

## 4.3 Internal Memory Layout: `std::list` (Doubly Linked)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD[Head Sentinel Node] --> N1[Node 1: prev=HEAD, data=10, next=Node2]
    N1 --> N2[Node 2: prev=Node1, data=20, next=Node3]
    N2 --> N3[Node 3: prev=Node2, data=30, next=Tail]
    N3 --> TAIL[Tail Sentinel Node]
    TAIL -.prev ptr.-> N3
    N3 -.prev ptr.-> N2
    N2 -.prev ptr.-> N1
    N1 -.prev ptr.-> HEAD
```

## 4.4 Internal Memory Layout: `std::deque`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAP[Central Map Array of Block Pointers]
    MAP --> B1[Block 1: 4 elements]
    MAP --> B2[Block 2: 4 elements]
    MAP --> B3[Block 3: 4 elements partially used]
    B1 --> E1[Index 0 to 3]
    B2 --> E2[Index 4 to 7]
    B3 --> E3[Index 8 to 9]
    E3 --> FREE[Unused Block Slots]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Flow: `push_back` Reallocation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[push_back called] --> B{Is size equal to capacity}
    B -- No --> C[Place element at end]
    C --> Z[Increment size, return]
    B -- Yes --> D[Allocate new block of size 2 times capacity]
    D --> E[Copy or move all old elements to new block]
    E --> F[Deallocate old block]
    F --> G[Update internal pointers beg, end, cap]
    G --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Expectation:** For 14-mark design questions, the examiner expects a hand-drawn or printed diagram of the container's internal memory layout. Practice drawing the **vector block**, the **doubly linked list node**, and the **deque map-of-blocks** structure. These three diagrams cover ~80% of all diagram marks in this module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between `std::vector` and `std::list` in terms of memory layout, random access, and iterator stability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | `std::vector` | `std::list` |
|---|---|---|
| Memory layout | Single contiguous heap block | Heap-allocated nodes linked by pointers |
| Random access | Supported, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ via `operator[]` | Not supported, requires $\mathcal{O}(k)$ traversal |
| Iterator stability | Invalidated on reallocation / insert / erase | Fully stable; only erased iterators are invalidated |

*Key takeaway (1 mark):* A `vector` is cache-friendly and faster for traversal and push-back; a `list` is preferred for frequent insertions/deletions in the middle and when iterator stability is critical.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is meant by "amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$" complexity of `vector::push_back`? Justify in one sentence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$ means that although a single `push_back` may occasionally cost $\mathcal{O}(n)$ when the underlying array is reallocated, the cost of $n$ consecutive `push_back` calls averages out to a constant per operation, because reallocation happens only logarithmically many times. The mathematical proof uses a geometric series (shown in Section 3.1 of these notes), yielding a total cost of $n - 1$ for $n$ pushes, i.e., $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts typically worth **7 + 7** marks. Students are given an **internal choice** between two full questions (A or B). Both questions below are completely independent alternatives.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the internal architecture of `std::vector`. With a neat diagram, describe how geometric reallocation works. Derive mathematically that `push_back` is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete, well-commented C++ program using the STL to:
  - Read $n$ integers from the user.
  - Store them in a `std::vector<int>`.
  - Remove all even numbers using the `erase-remove` idiom.
  - Print the resulting vector using a range-based `for` loop and an `std::ostream_iterator`.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Internal Architecture of `std::vector`:**

A `std::vector` is implemented as a class template that maintains **three internal pointers** (or equivalently, a pointer and two sizes):
- `_M_start` (or `begin()`) — points to the first element.
- `_M_finish` (or `end()`) — points one-past the last element. `size() == end() - begin()`.
- `_M_end_of_storage` (or `capacity_end`) — points one-past the last allocated slot.

All three pointers address a **single contiguous block of memory** allocated on the heap.

**Reallocation Strategy (Geometric Growth):**

When `push_back` is called and `size() == capacity()`, the vector:
1. Allocates a new buffer of size $C_{\text{new}} = \alpha \cdot C_{\text{old}}$, where $\alpha = 2$ in most implementations (GCC's libstdc++ uses $\alpha = 2$; MSVC uses $\alpha \approx 1.5$).
2. **Copy-constructs** (or **move-constructs** in C++11+) each existing element into the new buffer.
3. Destroys the old elements and deallocates the old buffer.
4. Updates the three internal pointers.

**Diagram of the reallocation process:** Refer to Section 4.2 and 4.5 of these notes for the contiguous block and the reallocation flow.

**Derivation of Amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$:**

Let $n$ be the total number of `push_back` operations. The capacities visited during growth are:

$$
1, 2, 4, 8, \dots, n
$$

Total element moves across all reallocations is the sum of a geometric series:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} 2^{k} = 2^{\log_2 n} - 1 = n - 1
$$

Amortized cost per operation:

$$
\frac{T(n)}{n} = \frac{n - 1}{n} \approx 1 = \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

Hence `push_back` is amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **(7 marks: 2 for architecture + 2 for diagram + 3 for derivation)**

---

**(b) [7 marks] — C++ Program:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <iterator>
#include <algorithm>

int main() {
    int n;
    std::cout << "Enter number of integers: ";
    std::cin >> n;

    std::vector<int> v;
    v.reserve(n);  // Optional: pre-allocate to avoid reallocations

    std::cout << "Enter " << n << " integers: ";
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        int x;
        std::cin >> x;
        v.push_back(x);
    }

    // Erase-remove idiom: remove all even numbers
    v.erase(
        std::remove_if(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int x) { return x % 2 == 0; }),
        v.end()
    );

    // Print using range-based for and ostream_iterator
    std::cout << "Resulting vector: ";
    for (int x : v) {
        std::cout << x << " ";
    }
    std::cout << std::endl;

    std::cout << "Formatted output: ";
    std::copy(v.begin(), v.end(), std::ostream_iterator<int>(std::cout, ", "));
    std::cout << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation of key constructs (for marks):**
- `std::remove_if` partitions the vector; it does **not** change the container's size — it returns an iterator to the new "logical end."
- `v.erase(...)` actually removes the elements between the logical end and the real end.
- The lambda `[](int x) { return x % 2 == 0; }` is a **functor** (function object) that acts as the predicate.
- `std::ostream_iterator<int>` writes each element to `std::cout` separated by `", "`. **(7 marks: 2 for input + 2 for erase-remove + 2 for output + 1 for compilation/correctness)**

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare `std::list`, `std::forward_list`, and `std::deque` with respect to internal structure, time complexity of insertion at front/back/middle, memory overhead per element, and iterator category.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a C++ program that uses `std::list<int>` to maintain a sorted list of integers. Demonstrate the use of `push_back`, `push_front`, `sort`, `unique`, and `reverse` member functions. Show the list state after each operation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `std::list` | `std::forward_list` | `std::deque` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal structure | Doubly linked list of nodes | Singly linked list of nodes | Segmented array (map of blocks) |
| Insert at front | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Insert at back | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Insert in middle | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ at iterator | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ *after* iterator | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (shifts blocks) |
| Memory per element | 2 pointers + element | 1 pointer + element | Element only, with shared block overhead |
| Random access | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Iterator category | Bidirectional | Forward | Random Access |
| `size()` | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |

**Key points for full marks (7 marks: 1 per row + 1 for narrative conclusion):**
- `list` allows bidirectional traversal but pays the cost of two pointers per node.
- `forward_list` is more memory-efficient but has an asymmetric API (`insert_after` instead of `insert`).
- `deque` offers a unique blend: random access + $\mathcal{O}(1)$ at both ends, at the cost of slightly more complex internal structure and no stable iterators on middle insertions.

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Sorted List Program:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <list>
#include <iterator>

int main() {
    std::list<int> lst;

    // Demonstrate push_back and push_front
    lst.push_back(30);
    lst.push_back(10);
    lst.push_front(50);
    lst.push_front(20);
    lst.push_back(40);

    std::cout << "Initial list: ";
    std::copy(lst.begin(), lst.end(), std::ostream_iterator<int>(std::cout, " "));
    std::cout << std::endl;

    // Sort the list
    lst.sort();
    std::cout << "After sort:  ";
    std::copy(lst.begin(), lst.end(), std::ostream_iterator<int>(std::cout, " "));
    std::cout << std::endl;

    // Add duplicates
    lst.push_back(20);
    lst.push_back(40);

    // Remove consecutive duplicates
    lst.unique();
    std::cout << "After unique: ";
    std::copy(lst.begin(), lst.end(), std::ostream_iterator<int>(std::cout, " "));
    std::cout << std::endl;

    // Reverse the list
    lst.reverse();
    std::cout << "After reverse: ";
    std::copy(lst.begin(), lst.end(), std::ostream_iterator<int>(std::cout, " "));
    std::cout << std::endl;

    return 0;
}
```

**Expected Output Trace (essential for full marks):**

| Step | Operation | List State (left to right) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | After `push_back(30)` | 30 |
| 1 | After `push_back(10)` | 30, 10 |
| 2 | After `push_front(50)` | 50, 30, 10 |
| 3 | After `push_front(20)` | 20, 50, 30, 10 |
| 4 | After `push_back(40)` | 20, 50, 30, 10, 40 |
| 5 | After `lst.sort()` | 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 |
| 6 | After adding 20, 40 | 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 20, 40 |
| 7 | After `lst.unique()` | 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 20, 40 |
| 8 | After `lst.reverse()` | 40, 20, 50, 40, 30, 20, 10 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial note about `std::list::unique()`:** It removes **only consecutive** duplicates. The list **must be sorted first** if you want all duplicates removed. Many students lose marks here by writing "it removes all duplicates" — this is **wrong**. **(7 marks: 2 for code structure + 2 for trace + 2 for correct member-function usage + 1 for `unique` semantics explanation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 2 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> 1. **Forgetting `#include` headers.** `<vector>`, `<list>`, `<algorithm>`, `<iterator>` are all **separate** headers. Missing any of them = compilation error = 0 marks for that sub-part.
> 2. **Confusing `size()` and `capacity()`** of a `vector`. They are not the same. `size()` is the number of elements; `capacity()` is the size of the underlying storage.
> 3. **Claiming `std::list::unique()` removes *all* duplicates.** It removes only **consecutive** duplicates. The list must be sorted first.
> 4. **Using `std::sort` directly on a `std::list`.** It will not compile because `list` does not provide random-access iterators. You must use the **member function** `lst.sort()`.
> 5. **Saying "vector is faster than list"** without context. The correct phrasing is: "vector has better cache locality and is faster for traversal and push-back; list is faster only for insertions/deletions in the middle that don't require traversal."
> 6. **Forgetting the `std::` namespace prefix** in the exam. Either write `std::vector<int> v;` or use `using namespace std;` at the top of the program.
> 7. **In the amortized-cost derivation, forgetting to use a geometric series** and just writing "because reallocations are rare." That is **not a proof** — it is hand-waving and will fetch at most 1 out of 3 derivation marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: C++ STL Linear Data Structures**

- **STL = Containers + Iterators + Algorithms + Functors.** Know the role of each.
- **Five sequence containers:** `vector`, `deque`, `list`, `forward_list`, `array`. Know the internal structure and complexity of each.
- **`vector`** is a contiguous dynamic array; `push_back` is amortized $\mathcal{O}(1)$; reallocation invalidates *all* iterators and references.
- **`list`** is a doubly linked list; supports `push_front`, `push_back`, `sort` (member), `unique`, `reverse`, `remove`, `splice` — all in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ or $\mathcal{O}(n)$ as appropriate.
- **`deque`** is a segmented array; supports $\mathcal{O}(1)$ insertion/removal at **both** ends and $\mathcal{O}(1)$ random access.
- **`array`** is a fixed-size, zero-overhead wrapper over a C-array; use it whenever the size is known at compile time.
- **`forward_list`** is a singly linked list; uses `insert_after` / `erase_after`; cannot compute `size()` in $\mathcal{O}(1)$.
- **Iterator categories** (ascending capability): Input → Forward → Bidirectional → Random Access. `vector`, `deque`, `array` provide random access; `list` provides bidirectional; `forward_list` provides forward.
- **The erase-remove idiom:** `c.erase(std::remove_if(c.begin(), c.end(), pred), c.end());` — must be memorized.
- **Amortized cost proof:** For vector with doubling growth, total cost of $n$ pushes is $2n - 1 = \mathcal{O}(n)$, hence $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized per push.
- **Cache locality** is the hidden reason `vector` often beats `list` in practice, even when the asymptotic complexity of `list` looks better.
- **Use the right tool:** `vector` for default, `deque` for front+back access, `list` for stable iterators and middle insertions, `array` for fixed sizes, `forward_list` for memory-critical singly-linked needs.
- **Required headers:** `<vector>`, `<list>`, `<deque>`, `<array>`, `<forward_list>`, `<algorithm>`, `<iterator>`.
- **STL algorithms to know:** `sort`, `stable_sort`, `find`, `count`, `count_if`, `transform`, `copy`, `remove_if`, `for_each`, `accumulate`, `min_element`, `max_element`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Linear Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Linear Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — NON-LINEAR DATA STRUCTURES

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Learning Outcome (CO3):** The student should be able to **implement, apply, and analyze non-linear data structures such as Trees (Binary, BST, AVL, Heap) and Graphs (BFS/DFS) for efficient problem solving.**

---

## 1.1 What is a Non-Linear Data Structure?

A **Non-Linear Data Structure (NLDS)** is a data organization model in which elements are **not arranged in a sequential (linear) order**. Instead, data elements are connected in a **hierarchical** or **network-based** relationship, where one element can connect to **multiple predecessors and successors**.

### 🎯 Formal Definition (KTU Terminology)
> A data structure is called **non-linear** if its elements do not form a single sequence of `prev`/`next` transitions (as in arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues), but instead form a **multi-dimensional relationship** where the insertion, deletion, and traversal follow a *branching* pattern.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "Family Tree & Road Map"

Imagine two real-world objects:

| Linear Structure (Module 2) | Non-Linear Structure (Module 3) |
|---|---|
| A **single-lane queue** at a ration shop (FIFO) | A **family tree** with parents, children, grandparents |
| One predecessor → One successor | One element → Multiple successors |

- 🌳 A **Tree** is like a **family tree**: one ancestor (root) gives rise to many descendants; there is exactly **one path** between any two nodes.
- 🗺️ A **Graph** is like a **Google Map**: cities (nodes) are connected by roads (edges); you can have **multiple paths** and even **cycles** between two cities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - **Tree** = Acyclic + Connected + Hierarchical
> - **Graph** = May contain cycles + May be disconnected + Network model

---

## 1.2 Classification of Non-Linear Data Structures (NLDS)

```
                     Non-Linear Data Structures
                              │
              ┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
              │                               │
           TREES                            GRAPHS
              │                               │
    ┌─────────┼─────────┐             ┌────────┼────────┐
    │         │         │             │                 │
 Binary     BST       AVL         Directed         Undirected
 Tree                 Tree         (Digraph)        Graph
    │
  Heap
```

### 1.2.1 The Tree (Hierarchical Model)

A **Tree** is a finite set of one or more nodes such that:
1. There is a specially designated node called the **Root**.
2. The remaining nodes are partitioned into `n ≥ 0` disjoint subtrees `T₁, T₂, … Tₙ`.

**Tree Terminology — KTU Must-Know Vocabulary**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Root** | Topmost node (no parent) |
| **Parent** | Immediate predecessor of a node |
| **Child** | Immediate successor of a node |
| **Leaf / Terminal Node** | Node with **degree = 0** (no children) |
| **Internal Node** | Node with at least one child |
| **Sibling** | Nodes sharing the same parent |
| **Degree of a node** | Number of children of that node |
| **Degree of a tree** | $\max(\text{degree of all nodes})$ |
| **Depth of a node** | Length of path from root → that node |
| **Height of a tree** | $\max(\text{depth of all nodes})$ |
| **Edge** | Connection between parent and child |
| **Level** | All nodes at the same depth form a level |

### 1.2.2 The Graph (Network Model)

A **Graph** $G$ is mathematically defined as an ordered pair:
$$G = (V, E)$$

Where:
- $V$ = finite, non-empty set of **vertices (nodes)**
- $E$ = set of **edges** (pairs of vertices)

Two principal types:
- **Directed Graph (Digraph):** Edges have a direction; edge $\langle u, v \rangle \neq \langle v, u \rangle$.
- **Undirected Graph:** Edges are bidirectional; edge $(u, v) = (v, u)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why study NLDS in KTU?** Real engineering problems — file systems (trees), social networks (graphs), routing algorithms, database indexing (B-trees), priority scheduling (heaps) — all rely on non-linear organization for achieving **sub-linear time complexity** (often $O(\log n)$).
