---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Differentiate between stable and unstable sorting algorithms. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A sorting algorithm is **stable** if it preserves the relative order of records with equal keys in the input array. A sorting algorithm is **unstable** if it may rearrange the relative order of equal-keyed elements.

**Example of Stable:** Merge Sort, Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort.
**Example of Unstable:** Quick Sort (standard), Heap Sort, Selection Sort.

**Illustration:** Consider records `[(5, A), (3, B), (5, C), (1, D)]` sorted by first element. A stable sort will produce `[(1, D), (3, B), (5, A), (5, C)]` — note that `(5, A)` still comes before `(5, C)`. An unstable sort might produce `[(1, D), (3, B), (5, C), (5, A)]`.

Stability matters in **multi-key sorting** and in **database operations** where secondary order must be preserved. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Why does Quick Sort degrade to $O(n^2)$ in the worst case? Suggest two techniques to avoid this.**

**Model Answer:**

Quick Sort's worst case occurs when the **pivot selection** consistently produces highly unbalanced partitions, e.g., one sub-array of size $n-1$ and another of size $0$. This happens with already-sorted or reverse-sorted input when the first or last element is chosen as the pivot.

The recurrence becomes:
$$T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(n) = O(n^2)$$

**Techniques to avoid the worst case:**

1. **Randomized Pivot Selection:** Choose a random element as the pivot. The probability of consistently bad pivots becomes negligible, giving an **expected** $O(n \log n)$ runtime.
2. **Median-of-Three Pivot:** Use the median of the first, middle, and last elements as the pivot. This avoids pathological cases on sorted/reverse-sorted input.
3. **3-Way Partitioning (Dutch National Flag):** Groups elements equal to the pivot together, reducing work when many duplicates exist.

**(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### INTERNAL CHOICE — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the Merge Sort algorithm in detail. Trace it on the array `[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`. Show all intermediate merging steps. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Definition:** Merge Sort is a **divide-and-conquer** algorithm that recursively splits the array into two halves, sorts each half, and then merges the two sorted halves.

**Recursive Procedure:**

```
MergeSort(arr, left, right):
    if left >= right:
        return
    mid = (left + right) / 2
    MergeSort(arr, left, mid)
    MergeSort(arr, mid+1, right)
    Merge(arr, left, mid, right)
```

**Merge Procedure:** Uses two pointers to compare elements from the two sorted halves and place the smaller one into a temporary array, then copies the result back.

**Trace on `[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`:**

**Divide Tree:**
- `[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]` splits at index 3 into `[38, 27, 43, 3]` and `[9, 82, 10]`.
- `[38, 27, 43, 3]` splits at index 1 into `[38, 27]` and `[43, 3]`.
- `[9, 82, 10]` splits at index 5 into `[9, 82]` and `[10]`.
- `[38, 27]` splits into `[38]` and `[27]`.
- `[43, 3]` splits into `[43]` and `[3]`.
- `[9, 82]` splits into `[9]` and `[82]`.

**Merge Tree (Bottom-Up):**
1. `[38]` + `[27]` → `[27, 38]`
2. `[43]` + `[3]` → `[3, 43]`
3. `[9]` + `[82]` → `[9, 82]`
4. `[27, 38]` + `[3, 43]` → `[3, 27, 38, 43]`
5. `[9, 82]` + `[10]` → `[9, 10, 82]`
6. `[3, 27, 38, 43]` + `[9, 10, 82]` → `[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]`

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ in all cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Compare Quick Sort and Merge Sort in terms of time complexity, space complexity, stability, and best use cases. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Quick Sort | Merge Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **Best Case Time** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Average Time** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Worst Case Time** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(\log n)$ — recursion stack only | $O(n)$ — auxiliary merge array |
| **Stability** | Not stable (standard) | Stable |
| **In-Place** | Yes | No |
| **Best Use Case** | General-purpose in-memory sort | External sorting (disk-based), stable sort required |
| **Typical Use** | Default in many C/C++ `qsort` libraries | Sorting linked lists, large datasets that don't fit in RAM |

**Key Differences:**

1. **Worst-case guarantee:** Merge Sort guarantees $O(n \log n)$ always; Quick Sort degrades to $O(n^2)$ on bad pivots.
2. **Memory:** Quick Sort is in-place (only $O(\log n)$ stack); Merge Sort needs $O(n)$ auxiliary memory.
3. **Stability:** Merge Sort is stable; Quick Sort is not (in its basic form).
4. **Cache performance:** Quick Sort has better cache locality due to in-place partitioning; Merge Sort's auxiliary array causes more cache misses.

**Verdict:** For most general-purpose applications, Quick Sort is preferred for its speed and low memory overhead. For applications requiring **stability** or **guaranteed worst-case performance** (e.g., real-time systems), Merge Sort is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

### INTERNAL CHOICE — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the Heap Sort algorithm. Using the array `[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`, show the construction of the max-heap and the first three extraction steps. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Definition:** Heap Sort uses a **binary max-heap** data structure. It consists of two phases: (1) **Build Max-Heap** — rearrange the array into max-heap form; (2) **Extract Max** — repeatedly swap the root (maximum) with the last element of the heap and re-heapify the reduced heap.

**Heapify Procedure:** Given a node at index `i` with children at `2i+1` and `2i+2`, find the largest among the node and its children, and if the largest is a child, swap and recurse.

**Step 1: Build Max-Heap on `[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]`**

Initial tree:
```
            38
          /    \
        27      43
       /  \    /  \
      3    9  82   10
```

Apply `heapify` from index $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1 = 2$ down to 0:

- **Index 2 (value 43):** Children 82, 10. Largest is 82. Swap 43 and 82. Array becomes `[38, 27, 82, 3, 9, 43, 10]`.
- **Index 1 (value 27):** Children 3, 9. Largest is 9. Swap 27 and 9. Array becomes `[38, 9, 82, 3, 27, 43, 10]`.
- **Index 0 (value 38):** Children 9, 82. Largest is 82. Swap 38 and 82. Array becomes `[82, 9, 38, 3, 27, 43, 10]`. Now recursively heapify index 2 (value 38): children 43, 10. Swap 38 and 43. Array becomes `[82, 9, 43, 3, 27, 38, 10]`.

**Max-Heap built:** `[82, 9, 43, 3, 27, 38, 10]`

**Step 2: First Three Extractions**

**Extraction 1:** Swap 82 (root) with 10 (last). Heapify root on `[10, 9, 43, 3, 27, 38]`:
- Children 9, 43. Largest is 43. Swap 10 and 43 → `[43, 9, 10, 3, 27, 38, 82]`. Heapify index 2 (value 10): children 38, nothing. Swap 10 and 38 → `[43, 9, 38, 3, 27, 10, 82]`. Final heap: `[43, 9, 38, 3, 27, 10]`.

**Extraction 2:** Swap 43 with 10. Heapify root on `[10, 9, 38, 3, 27]`:
- Children 9, 38. Largest is 38. Swap 10 and 38 → `[38, 9, 10, 3, 27, 43, 82]`. Heapify index 2 (value 10): no larger child. Final heap: `[38, 9, 10, 3, 27]`.

**Extraction 3:** Swap 38 with 27. Heapify root on `[27, 9, 10, 3]`:
- Children 9, 10. Largest is 10. Swap 27 and 10 → `[10, 9, 27, 3, 38, 43, 82]`. Heapify index 2 (value 27): child 3. 27 > 3, no swap. Final heap: `[10, 9, 27, 3]`.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ in all cases. **Space:** $O(1)$ in-place. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Explain Radix Sort. Trace it on the array `[170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66]`. Why is stability important in Radix Sort? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Definition:** Radix Sort is a **non-comparison-based** sorting algorithm that processes integer keys **digit-by-digit**, from the **least significant digit (LSD)** to the **most significant digit (MSD)`. At each pass, a **stable** sub-sort (typically Counting Sort) is applied.

**Procedure:**
1. Find the maximum value to determine the number of digits `d`.
2. For each digit position `k` (units, tens, hundreds, ...):
   - Distribute elements into 10 buckets (0–9) by the current digit.
   - Collect the buckets in order to form the new array.

**Trace on `[170, 45, 75, 90, 802, 24, 2, 66]`:**

Maximum value = 802, so `d = 3`.

**Pass 1 (Units Digit):**
- Bucket 0: 170, 90
- Bucket 2: 802, 2
- Bucket 4: 24
- Bucket 5: 45, 75
- Bucket 6: 66

Collected: `[170, 90, 802, 2, 24, 45, 75, 66]`

**Pass 2 (Tens Digit):**
- Bucket 0: 802, 2, 24, 45, 75
- Bucket 3: 130 (none)
- Bucket 6: 66
- Bucket 7: 170
- Bucket 9: 90

Collected: `[802, 2, 24, 45, 75, 66, 170, 90]`

**Pass 3 (Hundreds Digit):**
- Bucket 0: 2, 24, 45, 66, 75, 90
- Bucket 1: 170
- Bucket 8: 802

Collected: `[2, 24, 45, 66, 75, 90, 170, 802]` ✓

**Why Stability is Critical:** Radix Sort works because each pass uses a **stable** sort. When sorting by the tens digit, the elements with the same tens digit (e.g., 2 and 24 both have tens digit 2) retain their **relative order from the previous pass** (where 2 came before 24 in the units-sorted array). Without stability, the previous pass's ordering would be destroyed, and the final result would be incorrect.

**Time Complexity:** $O(d \cdot (n + k))$ where `d` is the number of digits and `k = 10` (base). For this example: $3 \times (8 + 10) = 54$ operations. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing stable vs. in-place:** Stable means equal-keyed elements preserve order; in-place means $O(1)$ extra memory. Many students incorrectly mark Merge Sort as in-place.
> 2. **Forgetting auxiliary array in Merge Sort:** Writing $O(1)$ space for Merge Sort is the most common error.
> 3. **Quick Sort's worst case:** Stating the worst case is "always $O(n \log n)$" — it is $O(n^2)$ on already-sorted input with a bad pivot choice.
> 4. **Radix Sort limitation:** Forgetting that Radix Sort cannot directly sort negative numbers or floating-point numbers without modification.
> 5. **Heap Sort stability:** Stating Heap Sort is stable — it is not. The root-extraction process can swap a parent with a child, disturbing relative order.
> 6. **Not showing intermediate steps:** KTU examiners require **full traces** for trace questions. Skipping intermediate states costs 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Selection Sort swap count:** Writing "performs $O(n \log n)$ swaps" — it is exactly $n-1$ swaps.
> 8. **Master Theorem application:** When asked for recurrence solutions, write the Master Theorem **case** (1, 2, or 3) along with the result.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Sorting Algorithms**

- ✅ **Bubble Sort** is $O(n)$ best case (with early-termination flag) and $O(n^2)$ worst case. Stable, in-place, adaptive.
- ✅ **Insertion Sort** is $O(n)$ best case. Stable, in-place, adaptive. Best for small `n` and nearly-sorted data.
- ✅ **Selection Sort** is $O(n^2)$ in **all** cases. Performs exactly $n-1$ swaps. Not stable, not adaptive.
- ✅ **Quick Sort** is $O(n \log n)$ average, $O(n^2)$ worst. Not stable. In-place with $O(\log n)$ stack space. Faster in practice than Merge Sort due to cache locality.
- ✅ **Merge Sort** is $O(n \log n)$ in **all** cases. **Stable.** Requires $O(n)$ auxiliary memory. Preferred for external sort and linked list sort.
- ✅ **Heap Sort** is $O(n \log n)$ in all cases. **Not stable.** In-place with $O(1)$ space. Combines the best of both worlds asymptotically.
- ✅ **Radix Sort** is $O(n \cdot d)$ for `d`-digit integer keys. **Stable** and **non-comparison-based**. Cannot directly sort negative or floating-point numbers.
- ✅ The **comparison-based lower bound** is $\Omega(n \log n)$. Radix Sort bypasses this by not using comparisons.
- ✅ **Lomuto vs. Hoare partition:** Lomuto is simpler; Hoare is more efficient.
- ✅ **Build-heap is $O(n)$**, not $O(n \log n)$ — this is a common trap question.
- ✅ For **already-sorted** input, **Insertion Sort and Bubble Sort (with flag) achieve $O(n)$**; Quick Sort (with bad pivot) degrades to $O(n^2)$.
- ✅ **Stability matters** when sorting records with secondary keys (e.g., sort by name, then stably sort by salary).
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark answers, always include: (1) algorithm logic, (2) full trace, (3) recurrence relation, (4) complexity derivation, and (5) a comparison table.
- ✅ **Memorize the master comparison table** — it is the most frequently asked 14-mark question structure in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C++ STL Implementation of Advanced Data Structures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT612 - DATA STRUCTURES USING C++
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** C++ STL Implementation of Advanced Data Structures

# C++ STL Implementation of Advanced Data Structures

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Standard Template Library (STL)** is a powerful, generic, compile-time-polymorphic software library that is part of the ISO C++ Standard. It provides a collection of **four interconnected components**: *containers*, *iterators*, *algorithms*, and *function objects (functors)*. STL allows the programmer to write reusable, type-safe, and high-performance data structures and algorithms by leveraging **C++ templates**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For Module 3, the focus is on the *implementation perspective* — understanding how advanced data structures (Stack, Queue, Deque, Priority Queue, Set, Map, Multimap) are realized through STL container adapters and associative containers, along with iterator traversal and STL algorithm invocation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Carpenter's Toolbox"**
>
> Imagine you are a carpenter. Instead of cutting wood, hammering nails, and sawing planks from scratch for every piece of furniture, you reach into a well-organized **toolbox**:
> - **Containers** = The labelled drawers (one for screws, one for nails, one for planks). Each drawer stores items in its *own organized way* — sorted, unsorted, paired with labels, etc.
> - **Iterators** = The **handles / pointers** that let you reach into any drawer and pull out items one by one without caring about *how* the drawer is organized internally.
> - **Algorithms** = The **power tools** (drill, sander) that operate on the items regardless of which drawer you pull them from.
> - **Functors** = The **custom attachments** you can snap onto a power tool to change its behaviour (e.g., a sanding disc instead of a cutting disc).
>
> STL is exactly this — a ready-made, generic toolbox for data structures.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of STL

| Pillar | Role | Example |
|--------|------|---------|
| **Containers** | Store collections of objects | `vector`, `list`, `map`, `set` |
| **Iterators** | Provide uniform access to container elements | `vector::iterator`, `map::iterator` |
| **Algorithms** | Operate on ranges defined by iterators | `sort()`, `find()`, `count()` |
| **Functors** | Customizable behaviour for algorithms | `less<T>`, `greater<T>` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Header Files:** `#include <vector>`, `<list>`, `<deque>`, `<stack>`, `<queue>`, `<set>`, `<map>`, `<algorithm>`, `<functional>`, `<iterator>`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of STL Containers

STL containers fall into **four major categories**:

```
STL Containers
├── Sequence Containers      (linear ordering)
│     ├── vector  (dynamic array)
│     ├── list    (doubly linked list)
│     └── deque   (double-ended queue)
│
├── Container Adapters       (restricted-interface wrappers)
│     ├── stack    (LIFO — built on deque/vector/list)
│     ├── queue    (FIFO — built on deque)
│     └── priority_queue  (max-heap — built on vector)
│
├── Associative Containers   (sorted, tree-based — Red-Black tree)
│     ├── set / multiset
│     └── map / multimap
│
└── Unordered Associative    (hash-table based — C++11+)
      ├── unordered_set / unordered_multiset
      └── unordered_map / unordered_multimap
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Module 3 emphasizes *Container Adapters* and *Associative Containers* as "Advanced Data Structures" because they are non-trivial: stacks/queues are not primitive; they are implemented using underlying sequence containers and provide restricted interfaces.

## 2.2 Sequence Containers — Detailed Analysis

### A. `std::vector<T>`
- **Underlying structure:** Contiguous dynamic array.
- **Memory model:** Single heap-allocated block; resizing involves *allocation + copy/move*.
- **Growth factor:** Implementations typically double capacity (amortized O(1) push_back).
- **Random access:** `O(1)` via `operator[]`.
- **Insertion/Deletion at end:** Amortized **O(1)**.
- **Insertion/Deletion at middle or front:** **O(n)** due to shifting.

### B. `std::list<T>`
- **Underlying structure:** Doubly linked list.
- **Memory model:** Each node is a separate heap allocation (extra pointer overhead per node).
- **Random access:** **Not supported** — no `operator[]`.
- **Insertion/Deletion at any iterator position:** **O(1)** (after reaching the position).
- **Traversal:** Bidirectional iterators.

### C. `std::deque<T>`
- **Underlying structure:** Indexed array of *chunks* (a "page-table" of fixed-size arrays).
- **Random access:** **O(1)**.
- **Insertion/Deletion at both ends (front & back):** **O(1)**.
- **Insertion/Deletion in middle:** **O(n)**.

## 2.3 Container Adapters — Advanced Data Structures

### A. `std::stack<T>` — LIFO
- **Adapter over:** `deque<T>` by default; can be built on `vector<T>` or `list<T>`.
- **Supported operations:** `push()`, `pop()`, `top()`, `empty()`, `size()`.
- **Restriction:** No iterators, no random access — only the top element is accessible.
- **All operations:** **O(1)**.

### B. `std::queue<T>` — FIFO
- **Adapter over:** `deque<T>` by default.
- **Supported operations:** `push()` (back), `pop()` (front), `front()`, `back()`, `empty()`, `size()`.
- **All operations:** **O(1)**.

### C. `std::priority_queue<T>` — Max-Heap by Default
- **Adapter over:** `vector<T>` + a *heap algorithm* (`make_heap`, `push_heap`, `pop_heap`).
- **Default comparator:** `less<T>` → produces a **max-heap** (largest element on `top()`).
- **Supported operations:** `push()`, `pop()`, `top()`, `empty()`, `size()`.
- **Complexities:** `push()` → **O(log n)**, `pop()` → **O(log n)**, `top()` → **O(1)**.

## 2.4 Associative Containers — The Tree-Based Advanced Structures

### A. `std::set<T>` — Sorted Unique Keys
- **Underlying structure:** **Red-Black Tree** (self-balancing BST).
- **Property:** Elements are stored in **sorted order**, duplicates are **not allowed**.
- **Key operations:** `insert()`, `erase()`, `find()` → all **O(log n)**.
- **Iterators:** Bidirectional; traversal yields elements in sorted order.

### B. `std::multiset<T>` — Sorted with Duplicates
- **Same as set**, but allows multiple equal elements.

### C. `std::map<K, V>` — Sorted Key–Value Pairs
- **Underlying structure:** **Red-Black Tree**.
- **Property:** Keys are unique and sorted; each key maps to exactly one value.
- **Element type:** `std::pair<const Key, Value>`.
- **Complexities:** Insertion, deletion, lookup → **O(log n)**.

### D. `std::multimap<K, V>` — Sorted with Duplicate Keys
- **Same as map**, but a key can map to multiple values.

## 2.5 Iterators — The Glue

Iterators provide a **uniform interface** to traverse containers, just as pointers do for arrays.

### Iterator Categories (from weakest to strongest)

| Category | Capabilities | Containers |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Input** | Read once, forward only | `istream_iterator` |
| **Output** | Write once, forward only | `ostream_iterator` |
| **Forward** | Read/write, forward, multipass | `forward_list`, `unordered_*` |
| **Bidirectional** | + backward | `list`, `set`, `map` |
| **Random Access** | + jump by offset (`it + n`) | `vector`, `deque` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Many students confuse `set`/`map` iterators as random-access. They are **bidirectional only**, so `it + 5` is a compilation error. Use `std::advance(it, 5)` instead.

## 2.6 STL Algorithms — Brief Reference

Algorithms operate on **half-open iterator ranges** `[first, last)`.

| Algorithm | Purpose | Complexity |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| `sort(begin, end)` | Quicksort/Introsort | **O(n log n)** |
| `binary_search(begin, end, val)` | Requires sorted range | **O(log n)** |
| `find(begin, end, val)` | Linear search | **O(n)** |
| `count(begin, end, val)` | Frequency count | **O(n)** |
| `reverse(begin, end)` | In-place reverse | **O(n)** |
| `accumulate(begin, end, init)` | Sum (in `<numeric>`) | **O(n)** |
| `for_each(begin, end, fn)` | Apply function | **O(n)** |
| `transform(begin, end, dst, fn)` | Apply + store | **O(n)** |
| `min_element(begin, end)` | Locate minimum | **O(n)** |
| `max_element(begin, end)` | Locate maximum | **O(n)** |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Complexity Cheat Sheet

| Container | Insert | Delete | Access | Search |
|-----------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| `vector` | **O(n)** middle, **O(1)** end (amortized) | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| `list` | **O(1)** at iterator | **O(1)** at iterator | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |
| `deque` | **O(1)** at both ends | **O(1)** at both ends | **O(1)** | **O(n)** |
| `stack` | **O(1)** push | **O(1)** pop | **O(1)** top | — |
| `queue` | **O(1)** push | **O(1)** pop | **O(1)** front/back | — |
| `priority_queue` | **O(log n)** push | **O(log n)** pop | **O(1)** top | — |
| `set` / `map` | **O(log n)** | **O(log n)** | — | **O(log n)** |
| `multiset` / `multimap` | **O(log n)** | **O(log n)** | — | **O(log n)** |
| `unordered_set` / `unordered_map` | avg **O(1)**, worst **O(n)** | avg **O(1)**, worst **O(n)** | — | avg **O(1)**, worst **O(n)** |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **`priority_queue`** → Dijkstra's shortest path, Huffman encoding, task scheduling in OS kernels, real-time event simulation.
- **`map` / `set`** → Symbol tables in compilers, database indexing, file-system directory structures, configuration parsers.
- **`stack`** → Function call stack, expression evaluation, parenthesis matching, undo/redo in editors, backtracking algorithms.
- **`queue`** → BFS traversals, OS process scheduling, producer–consumer problems, network packet buffering.
- **`deque`** → Sliding window algorithms, work-stealing schedulers, history buffers (browser back-button).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstration 1 — `std::stack<T>` from Scratch (Conceptual Derivation)

The KTU board often asks: *"Explain how `std::stack` is implemented internally."*

**Logical steps to derive a stack from a `deque`:**

1. A *stack* is a LIFO discipline. The operations we need are:
   - `push(x)` — insert at one end (call it the "top").
   - `pop()` — remove from that same end.
   - `top()` — read that end without removing.
2. We need an underlying container that supports **O(1) insertion AND deletion at one end** and **O(1) access to that end's element**.
3. Candidate analysis:
   - `vector` → O(1) push_back, O(1) back, O(1) pop_back. ✅ Works.
   - `deque` → O(1) push_back/push_front, O(1) front/back, O(1) pop_back/pop_front. ✅ Works.
   - `list` → O(1) push_back, O(1) back, O(1) pop_back. ✅ Works.
4. STL chooses `deque<T>` as the **default** because it offers the best balance: no reallocation churn like `vector`, no per-node allocation overhead like `list`.

**The conceptual "implementation" (showing intent, not full STL source):**

```cpp
template <class T, class Container = std::deque<T>>
class stack {
    Container c;       // underlying container
public:
    void push(const T& x)   { c.push_back(x); }   // top is the back
    void pop()              { c.pop_back(); }
    T&   top()              { return c.back(); }
    bool empty() const      { return c.empty(); }
    std::size_t size() const { return c.size(); }
};
```

**Line-by-line reasoning:**

- `c.push_back(x)` — chosen as `push` because `deque::push_back` is **O(1)** amortized.
- `c.pop_back()` — `deque::pop_back` is **O(1)** (no shifting of elements, unlike `vector` erase).
- `c.back()` — constant time access to the last element.
- `empty()` and `size()` are inherited as **O(1)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Because the adapter delegates all storage to the underlying container, a stack inherits the iterator behaviour of that container — *but* the adapter **deliberately hides iterators** to enforce the LIFO contract.

## 3.2 Demonstration 2 — Deriving the Complexity of `priority_queue::push()`

**Given:** A `priority_queue` is implemented as a **binary max-heap** stored in a `vector<T>` (a complete binary tree in level-order).

**Operations of `push(T x)`:**
1. **Append** `x` to the end of the vector → O(1).
2. Call `push_heap(c.begin(), c.end(), less<T>())` → this performs *sift-up* / *bubble-up*.

**Analysis of sift-up:**
- After insertion, `x` is at index `n-1`. Its parent is at index `(n-1-1)/2 = (n-2)/2`.
- If `x > parent`, swap them. Repeat until root or no violation.
- In the worst case, `x` travels from a leaf to the root.
- The height of a complete binary tree of `n` nodes is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.
- Therefore, the number of swaps is at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.

$$\text{Worst-case time of } push\_heap = O(\log n)$$

$$\text{Therefore } priority\_queue::push() = O(1) + O(\log n) = O(\log n)$$

Similarly for `pop()`:
1. Move last element to root → O(1).
2. Call `pop_heap` → *sift-down* from root to leaf → at most $\log n$ swaps.

$$priority\_queue::pop() = O(\log n)$$

And for `top()`:
- It simply returns `c.front()`, i.e., the root of the heap.

$$priority\_queue::top() = O(1)$$

## 3.3 Demonstration 3 — Building and Traversing a `std::map<K,V>`

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <map>
#include <string>
int main() {
    std::map<std::string, int> age;
    age["Alice"] = 30;
    age["Bob"]   = 25;
    age["Charlie"] = 35;
    age.insert({"David", 28});

    // Iteration yields elements in sorted order of KEY
    std::cout << "Sorted by key:\n";
    for (const auto& p : age) {
        std::cout << p.first << " -> " << p.second << "\n";
    }

    auto it = age.find("Bob");
    if (it != age.end()) {
        std::cout << "Found: " << it->first << " = " << it->second << "\n";
    }
    return 0;
}
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. `age["Alice"] = 30;` — If `"Alice"` does not exist, `operator[]` default-constructs the value (int → 0) and assigns 30. This is **O(log n)**.
2. `age.insert({"David", 28});` — Inserts a `pair<const string, int>`. **O(log n)**.
3. Range-based `for` loop desugars to iterator traversal. Since `map` iterators are **bidirectional**, the loop visits elements in **ascending key order** — a direct consequence of the Red-Black tree invariant.
4. `age.find("Bob")` — Performs a tree search in **O(log n)**. Returns `end()` if not found.

**Output:**

```
Sorted by key:
Alice -> 30
Bob -> 25
Charlie -> 35
David -> 28
Found: Bob = 25
```

## 3.4 Demonstration 4 — Custom Comparator with `priority_queue` (Min-Heap)

By default, `priority_queue` is a max-heap. To get a min-heap, supply `std::greater<T>` as the comparator.

```cpp
#include <queue>
#include <vector>
#include <functional>
#include <iostream>

int main() {
    std::priority_queue<int, std::vector<int>, std::greater<int>> minHeap;
    minHeap.push(50);
    minHeap.push(10);
    minHeap.push(30);
    minHeap.push(20);
    minHeap.push(40);

    std::cout << "Min-Heap extraction order:\n";
    while (!minHeap.empty()) {
        std::cout << minHeap.top() << " ";
        minHeap.pop();
    }
    // Output: 10 20 30 40 50
}
```

**Line-by-line reasoning:**

- Template parameters: `<T, Container, Compare>`. We pass `std::greater<int>` instead of the default `std::less<int>`.
- The comparator `greater<int>(a, b)` returns `true` when `a > b`. This inverts the heap property so the *smallest* element bubbles to the top.
- Extraction yields elements in **ascending order** — a perfect use-case for tasks like merging `k` sorted lists.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Container (KTU-Favorite Table)

| Requirement | Recommended Container | Reason |
|-------------|-----------------------|--------|
| Frequent random access, mostly push_back | `vector` | Cache-friendly, O(1) random access |
| Frequent insertions/deletions in middle | `list` | O(1) splice/erase at iterator |
| Need both front and back O(1) access | `deque` | Both ends O(1), still random access |
| LIFO discipline (e.g., DFS, recursion simulation) | `stack` | Restricted interface enforces LIFO |
| FIFO discipline (e.g., BFS) | `queue` | Restricted interface enforces FIFO |
| Always need the smallest/largest element quickly | `priority_queue` | O(log n) insert, O(1) min/max |
| Sorted unique elements, fast lookup | `set` | O(log n) all operations |
| Key-value pairs sorted by key | `map` | O(log n) all operations |
| Sorted elements with duplicates | `multiset` | Same as set, allows duplicates |
| Fastest average-case lookup, no order needed | `unordered_map` | O(1) average |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — STL Architecture Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[STL - Standard Template Library] --> B[Containers]
    A --> C[Iterators]
    A --> D[Algorithms]
    A --> E[Functors]

    B --> B1[Sequence: vector, list, deque]
    B --> B2[Adapters: stack, queue, priority_queue]
    B --> B3[Associative: set, multiset, map, multimap]
    B --> B4[Unordered: unordered_set, unordered_map]

    C --> C1[Input / Output]
    C --> C2[Forward]
    C --> C3[Bidirectional]
    C --> C4[Random Access]

    D --> D1[Non-mutating: find, count]
    D --> D2[Mutating: sort, reverse, transform]
    D --> D3[Numeric: accumulate]

    E --> E1[Arithmetic: plus, minus]
    E --> E2[Comparison: less, greater]
    E --> E3[Logical: logical_and, logical_or]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Container Adapter Internals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StackAdapter
        S1[std::stack T] --> S2[Underlying: deque T default]
        S2 --> S3[Operations: push, pop, top]
    end

    subgraph QueueAdapter
        Q1[std::queue T] --> Q2[Underlying: deque T default]
        Q2 --> Q3[Operations: push, pop, front, back]
    end

    subgraph PriorityQueueAdapter
        P1[std::priority_queue T] --> P2[Underlying: vector T]
        P2 --> P3[Heap Algorithm: make_heap, push_heap, pop_heap]
        P3 --> P4[Operations: push, pop, top]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Iterator Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I0[Iterator - root concept] --> I1[Input Iterator]
    I0 --> I2[Output Iterator]
    I1 --> I3[Forward Iterator]
    I3 --> I4[Bidirectional Iterator]
    I4 --> I5[Random Access Iterator]
    I5 --> I6[Contiguous Iterator - C++17]

    I1 -.used by.-> A1[istream_iterator]
    I2 -.used by.-> A2[ostream_iterator]
    I3 -.used by.-> A3[forward_list, unordered_set]
    I4 -.used by.-> A4[list, set, map, multimap]
    I5 -.used by.-> A5[vector, deque, array]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Associative Container Structure (Red-Black Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N40[Root: 40 - black] --> N20[20 - black]
    N40 --> N60[60 - red]
    N20 --> N10[10 - red]
    N20 --> N30[30 - black]
    N60 --> N50[50 - black]
    N60 --> N70[70 - black]
    N10 --> N5[5 - black]
    N10 --> N15[15 - black]

    style N40 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N20 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N60 fill:#c33,color:#fff
    style N10 fill:#c33,color:#fff
    style N30 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N50 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N70 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N5 fill:#222,color:#fff
    style N15 fill:#222,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Red-Black tree ensures the longest root-to-leaf path is at most **2× the shortest**, guaranteeing $O(\log n)$ height. This is why all `set`/`map` operations are logarithmic.

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Flow of an STL Algorithm

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Code
    participant C as Container
    participant IT as Iterators
    participant ALG as STL Algorithm
    participant FN as Functor

    U->>C: Create container
    U->>C: Insert data
    U->>IT: c.begin(), c.end()
    U->>ALG: sort(begin, end, functor)
    ALG->>IT: Iterate through range
    ALG->>FN: Compare elements via functor
    FN-->>ALG: Return comparison result
    ALG-->>U: Sorted container
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `std::stack` and `std::queue` in C++ STL. Mention the underlying container used by each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | `std::stack` | `std::queue` |
|---------|--------------|--------------|
| Discipline | **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) | **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) |
| Insertion | `push()` adds to top | `push()` adds to back |
| Removal | `pop()` removes from top | `pop()` removes from front |
| Access | `top()` returns top element | `front()` / `back()` |
| Default underlying container | **`std::deque<T>`** | **`std::deque<T>`** |
| Iterators | Not provided | Not provided |

Both are **container adapters** that restrict the interface of an underlying sequence container. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between `std::set` and `std::unordered_set`? Compare their search complexities.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- `std::set` is an **ordered associative container** implemented using a **Red-Black Tree**. Elements are stored in **sorted order**, and duplicates are **not allowed**. Search, insert, and delete all take **O(log n)** time.
- `std::unordered_set` is implemented using a **hash table**. Elements are stored in **no particular order**, and duplicates are **not allowed**. Search, insert, and delete take **average O(1)** time but **worst-case O(n)** time (in case of hash collisions).
- Use `set` when you need ordered traversal; use `unordered_set` when you need the fastest average-case lookup. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (e.g., (a) and (b)). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B as full internal choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the different categories of STL containers with suitable examples. Discuss the time complexity of basic operations on `vector`, `list`, and `deque`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Categories of STL Containers (4 marks):**

1. **Sequence Containers** — maintain linear ordering. Examples: `vector`, `list`, `deque`, `array`, `forward_list`.
2. **Container Adapters** — provide restricted interface over sequence containers. Examples: `stack`, `queue`, `priority_queue`.
3. **Associative Containers** — store sorted elements using Red-Black tree. Examples: `set`, `multiset`, `map`, `multimap`.
4. **Unordered Associative Containers** — store elements in hash tables (C++11). Examples: `unordered_set`, `unordered_map`, `unordered_multiset`, `unordered_multimap`.

**Complexity Table (3 marks):**

| Operation | `vector` | `list` | `deque` |
|-----------|----------|--------|---------|
| Random access `v[i]` | **O(1)** | Not supported | **O(1)** |
| Insert/delete at front | **O(n)** | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Insert/delete at back | **O(1)** amortized | **O(1)** | **O(1)** |
| Insert/delete in middle | **O(n)** | **O(1)** at iterator | **O(n)** |
| Search (linear) | **O(n)** | **O(n)** | **O(n)** |

**Conclusion:** Choose `vector` for random access, `list` for middle insertions, and `deque` for both-end operations with random access.

---

**(b)** Write a C++ program using STL to read `N` integers into a `vector`, sort them in descending order using `std::sort` with a custom comparator (functor), and display the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Program:**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
#include <functional>

// Custom functor for descending order
class Descending {
public:
    bool operator()(int a, int b) const {
        return a > b;  // returns true if a should come before b
    }
};

int main() {
    int n;
    std::cout << "Enter N: ";
    std::cin >> n;

    std::vector<int> v;
    std::cout << "Enter " << n << " integers:\n";
    for (int i = 0; i < n; ++i) {
        int x; std::cin >> x;
        v.push_back(x);
    }

    // Sort using custom functor
    std::sort(v.begin(), v.end(), Descending());

    std::cout << "Sorted in descending order:\n";
    for (int x : v) std::cout << x << " ";
    std::cout << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

**Explanation (mark-wise):**

- Includes and class definition: **2 marks**
- Input loop with `push_back`: **1 mark**
- `std::sort` with custom functor: **2 marks**
- Output display: **1 mark**
- Correctness and indentation: **1 mark**

**Alternative using `std::greater<int>()` (built-in functor, valid for full marks):**

```cpp
std::sort(v.begin(), v.end(), std::greater<int>());
```

**Sample I/O:**

```
Enter N: 5
Enter 5 integers: 4 1 7 3 9
Sorted in descending order: 9 7 4 3 1
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of iterators in C++ STL. List the five categories of iterators and the operations supported by each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Iterators are generalized pointers that provide a uniform way to access elements of any STL container, decoupling algorithms from container internals. They act as the *glue* between containers and algorithms.

**Five Categories (5 marks):**

| Category | Operations Supported | Container |
|----------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Input** | `*it`, `it++`, `it == it2`, `it != it2` (read once) | `istream_iterator` |
| **Output** | `*it = v`, `it++` (write once) | `ostream_iterator` |
| **Forward** | All of Input/Output, plus multipass traversal | `forward_list`, `unordered_set` |
| **Bidirectional** | All of Forward, plus `--it` | `list`, `set`, `map` |
| **Random Access** | All of Bidirectional, plus `it + n`, `it - n`, `it[n]`, `it1 - it2`, `it1 < it2` | `vector`, `deque`, `array` |

**Key Insight:** A higher category is a *strict superset* of the lower. An algorithm that needs only forward iteration works on any container. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Write a C++ program using STL `priority_queue` to merge `K` sorted integer vectors into a single sorted output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer Program (using min-heap):**

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <queue>
#include <functional>

// Structure to hold value, list index, and element index
struct Node {
    int value;
    int listIdx;
    int elemIdx;
    // Min-heap comparator: smaller value has higher priority
    bool operator>(const Node& other) const {
        return value > other.value;
    }
};

int main() {
    int k;
    std::cout << "Enter number of sorted lists (K): ";
    std::cin >> k;

    std::vector<std::vector<int>> lists(k);
    for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        int n;
        std::cout << "Size of list " << i << ": ";
        std::cin >> n;
        std::cout << "Enter " << n << " sorted elements: ";
        lists[i].resize(n);
        for (int j = 0; j < n; ++j) std::cin >> lists[i][j];
    }

    // Min-heap: smallest element on top
    std::priority_queue<Node, std::vector<Node>, std::greater<Node>> pq;

    // Push the first element of each list
    for (int i = 0; i < k; ++i) {
        if (!lists[i].empty()) {
            pq.push({lists[i][0], i, 0});
        }
    }

    std::cout << "Merged sorted output:\n";
    while (!pq.empty()) {
        Node cur = pq.top(); pq.pop();
        std::cout << cur.value << " ";

        if (cur.elemIdx + 1 < (int)lists[cur.listIdx].size()) {
            pq.push({lists[cur.listIdx][cur.elemIdx + 1], cur.listIdx, cur.elemIdx + 1});
        }
    }
    std::cout << "\n";
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark Distribution:**

- Struct definition and comparator: **2 marks**
- Min-heap initialization with first elements: **2 marks**
- Main merge loop: **2 marks**
- Output formatting: **1 mark**

**Complexity Analysis:** Total elements = $N$. Heap size = $K$. Each push/pop = $O(\log K)$.

$$T(N, K) = N \cdot O(\log K) = O(N \log K)$$

This is asymptotically optimal and **far better** than naive $O(NK)$ merging.

**Sample I/O:**

```
Enter number of sorted lists (K): 3
Size of list 0: 3   Enter: 1 4 7
Size of list 1: 2   Enter: 2 5
Size of list 2: 4   Enter: 3 6 8 9
Merged sorted output: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing `stack` iterator support** — Many students write `for(auto x : mystack)`, which is a **compilation error**. Adapters expose NO iterators. (-1 to -2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting `#include <functional>`** when using `std::greater<T>` or `std::less<T>`. The code may compile on some compilers but is not portable. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Calling `priority_queue::top()` on an empty queue** → undefined behaviour. Always check `empty()` first. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Stating `set::find()` is O(1)** — it is O(log n). Only `unordered_set::find()` is O(1) average. (-2 marks for incorrect complexity)
> 5. **Confusing `map::operator[]` with `insert`** — `operator[]` will *overwrite* the existing value, while `insert` will *not* overwrite. This is a frequent interview/KTU trap. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Writing `vector.begin() + 5` for a `list`** — compilation error. `list` iterators are bidirectional, not random. Use `std::advance(it, 5)`. (-1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- ⭐ **STL has 4 components:** Containers, Iterators, Algorithms, Functors.
- ⭐ **Sequence containers:** `vector` (random access O(1)), `list` (no random access, O(1) insert anywhere), `deque` (O(1) at both ends + random access).
- ⭐ **Container adapters** (`stack`, `queue`, `priority_queue`) **do not expose iterators** — this is a frequent KTU MCQ/trap.
- ⭐ **`stack` is LIFO**, **`queue` is FIFO**, **`priority_queue` is a max-heap by default** (use `std::greater<T>` for min-heap).
- ⭐ **`priority_queue` complexity:** `push` O(log n), `pop` O(log n), `top` O(1).
- ⭐ **Associative containers** (`set`, `map`, `multiset`, `multimap`) use a **Red-Black Tree** → all operations **O(log n)**, and traversal is always in **sorted key order**.
- ⭐ **Unordered containers** use a **hash table** → average **O(1)** but worst **O(n)**.
- ⭐ **Iterators** decouple algorithms from containers; categories are Input → Output → Forward → Bidirectional → Random Access → Contiguous.
- ⭐ **`map::operator[]` inserts a default value if the key is missing** (subtle but heavily tested).
- ⭐ **STL algorithms** operate on half-open ranges `[first, last)` and take optional comparator functors.
- ⭐ **Common algorithms to memorize:** `sort`, `find`, `count`, `reverse`, `accumulate`, `transform`, `min_element`, `max_element`, `binary_search`.
- ⭐ **Header files** (very common 2-mark question): `<vector>`, `<list>`, `<deque>`, `<stack>`, `<queue>`, `<set>`, `<map>`, `<algorithm>`, `<functional>`, `<iterator>`.
- ⭐ **Functor:** any object with `operator()` defined — passed to algorithms to customize behaviour.
- ⭐ **Time complexity questions are the most frequently asked 7–8 mark questions** in this module.
